paper title: Shadowless Projection Mapping for Tabletop Workspaces with Synthetic Aperture Projector
author list: Takahiro Okamoto, Masaki Takeuchi, Masataka Sawayama, Daisuke Iwai
abstract: Conventional projection mapping (PM) often produce distracting cast shadows when users occlude the light path. We present a synthetic-aperture PM system that densely arranges many projectors to achieve delay-free, shadowless projection without computational occlusion compensation. To mitigate spatial resolution loss from subpixel misalignment across overlaid projections, we develop and validate an offline blur compensation method whose computation time is independent of the number of projectors. We also show that shadowless PM is critical for material appearance manipulation without evoking projection-like impression. We define this as "sense of projection (SoP)" and propose a PM design framework based on user studies to minimize the SoP.

paper title: High-Contrast Projection Mapping under Light Field Illumination with LED Display and Aperiodic Lens Array
author list: Kotaro Fujimura, Hiroki Kusuyama, Masaki Takeuchi, Daisuke Iwai
abstract: We propose target-excluding lighting to overcome dark-room constraints in Projection Mapping (PM). Combining an LED display with an optimized aperiodic lens array, our system achieves light field illumination selectively lighting the environment while avoiding the PM target. This compact architecture provides a large effective light source area, reproducing natural soft shadows comparable to typical lighting, maintaining spatial controllability for precise avoidance. We introduce a computational technique optimizing aperiodic lens placement to suppress crosstalk-induced dark spots, and efficient methods for computing LED luminance patterns. Experiments with our prototype demonstrate high-contrast, dynamic PM in bright environments.

paper title: DiffPC: Diffusion-Based Projector Photometric Compensation
author list: Yuxi Wang, Haibin Ling, Bingyao Huang
abstract: Projector photometric compensation aims to mitigate color distortions introduced by surface textures, irregular material reflectance, and environmental lighting. Since existing deep-learning methods rely on laborious scene-specific data and often overlook perceptual quality, this paper presents a diffusion-based framework that reformulates compensation as a constrained denoising task. By modeling distortions as environment-dependent additive noise, this paper iteratively recovers compensation images using photometry-aware and content conditions. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach has strong generalization capabilities and achieves superior perceptual visual quality over prior art.

paper title: Setup-Independent Full Projector Compensation
author list: Haibo Li, Qingyue Deng, Jijiang Li, Haibin Ling, Bingyao Huang
abstract: Projector compensation aims to correct distortions on nonplanar or textured surfaces, but most methods require retraining whenever the environment changes. We introduce SIComp, the first setup-independent framework that generalizes to unseen setups without fine-tuning, supported by a new real-world dataset of 277 distinct configurations. By decoupling geometry and photometry through a tailored optical flow module and a novel photometric network with intensity-varying surface priors, SIComp consistently delivers high-quality compensation across diverse environments, establishing the first truly generalizable solution in the field.

paper title: ProCap: Projection-Aware Captioning for Spatial Augmented Reality
author list: Zimo Cao, Yuchen Deng, Haibin Ling, Bingyao Huang
abstract: Spatial Augmented Reality (SAR) faces "virtual-physical ambiguity," where Vision Language Models confuse projected content with physical environments. We propose ProCap, a framework that decouples these layers via automated segmentation and region-aware retrieval to mitigate projection distortion. We also introduce RGBP, the first large-scale SAR semantic dataset with 180,000+ projections and dense annotations. By using a dual-captioning protocol, ProCap enables independent reasoning of physical and virtual contexts, establishing a robust foundation for intelligent SAR interaction. The source code, pre-trained models and the RGBP dataset are available on the project page:  https://ZimoCao.github.io/ProCap/.

paper title: Eliciting Pleasantness with Haptic Feedback: The Role of Physical and Pseudo-Haptic Resistance in Virtual Archery
author list: Yusuke Ujitoko, Yuki Ban
abstract: Although haptic technologies that allow users to touch virtual objects have been actively researched in the academic field, they are not yet widely adopted in real-world applications. To promote broader adoption, designing haptic stimuli that elicit pleasant sensations is essential since hedonic motivation significantly drives user acceptance. However, how such pleasantness can be effectively induced through haptic stimulation during a user's active exploration remains an open question. In this study, we investigated the relationship between the applied force during user actions and the resulting pleasantness, using a bow-dragging and arrow-shooting scenario. Specifically, we conducted three psychophysical experiments to investigate two types of force feedback. One type was a physical force that stimulated the kinesthetic sense, and the other was a pseudo force induced through visual manipulation. Our results revealed that pleasantness tended to be elicited when physical resistance force was present during bow dragging, whereas this tendency was not observed for pseudo resistance force. Our findings show that the two types of force are not interchangeable for eliciting positive affect. We also found that the arrow's flying speed contributed to pleasantness as much as the physical resistance force, suggesting that pleasantness is influenced postdictively. Moreover, across all experiments, perceived pleasantness was strongly correlated with the perceived naturalness of the bow-and-arrow behavior. These findings provide valuable insights for designing pleasant interactions involving continuous force feedback, highlighting the distinct roles of physical and pseudo-haptic cues.

paper title: Move or Push? Studying Pseudo-Haptic Perceptions Obtained with Motion or Force Input
author list: Yutaro Hirao, Takuji Narumi, Ferran Argelaguet, Anatole Lécuyer
abstract: Pseudo-haptics techniques are interesting alternatives for inducing haptic perceptions, achieved by manipulating haptic perception through the appropriate alteration of primarily visual feedback in response to body movements. However, the use of pseudo-haptics techniques with a motion-input system can sometimes be limited. This paper investigates a novel approach for extending the potential of pseudo-haptics techniques in virtual reality (VR), focusing on pseudoweight perception as the target case. The proposed approach utilizes a reaction force from force-input as a substitution of haptic cue for the pseudo-haptic perception. The paper introduced a manipulation method in which the vertical acceleration of the virtual hand is controlled by the extent of push-in of a force sensor. Such a force-input manipulation of a virtual body cannot only present pseudo-haptics with smaller physical spaces and be used by various users including physically handicapped people, but can present the reaction force proportional to the user's input to the user. We hypothesized that such a haptic force cue would contribute to the pseudo-haptic, here, the pseudoweight perception. Therefore, the paper endeavors to investigate the force-input pseudo-haptic perception in comparison with the motion-input pseudo-haptics. The paper compared force-input and motion-input manipulation in a point of achievable range and resolution of pseudo-weight. The experimental results suggest that forceinput manipulation successfully extends the range of perceptible pseudo-weight by 80% in comparison to the motion-input manipulation. On the other hand, it is revealed that motion-input manipulation has 1 step larger number of distinguishable weight levels and is easier to operate.

paper title: AsymDrive: Fingertip-Free Force Illusions Induced by Asymmetric Vibrations on Dorsal-Palmar Tendons
author list: Koki Fukuda, Kazuma Aoyama, Hideaki Kuzuoka, Takuji Narumi, Tomohiro Amemiya
abstract: Force feedback to the hand is essential for enhancing immersion in VR and facilitating skill transfer. However, no existing method allows unconstrained hand and arm positions while keeping the fingertips free. We present a novel fingertip-free technique that induces directional force illusions on the entire hand by applying asymmetric vibrations to antagonistic dorsal and palmar tendons. Through two user studies, we demonstrate that directional accuracy is significantly above chance level under dual-point asymmetric vibration, in contrast single-point or symmetric vibration. By integrating effective haptic feedback with unconstrained hand interaction, our approach offers a promising avenue toward richer VR experiences.

paper title: "Feeling" the Stretch: Enhancing Embodiment in Virtual Arm Elongation through Spatiotemporal Modulation of Visio-Haptic Feedback
author list: Sara Rossi, Claudio Pacchierotti, Maud Marchal
abstract: The realism of virtual body illusions depends on congruent sensory feedback. We explored the effects of visio-haptic stimulation on the sense of embodiment in VR. The first experiment identified optimal vibrotactile parameters during an 18s, 40% arm elongation illusion, finding that sequential stimulation along the full arm with 500ms motor activation was most effective. From this, we derived a formula to generalize the optimal haptic timing based on elongation velocity. The formula was then validated in two follow-up experiments: one varying the elongation percentage and another varying the elongation duration. Our findings offer validated guidelines for designing more immersive haptic experiences for virtual body deformations.

paper title: Event-Based Referred Vibrotactile Feedback for Bare-Hand XR Interaction
author list: Juyoung Lee, Hyunseo Seo, Hyunjin Lee, Minju Baeck, Hui-Shyong Yeo, Woontack Woo
abstract: We propose event-based vibrotactile feedback for enhancing bare-hand XR interaction through commodity smartwatches. Unlike controllers and haptic gloves that require additional hardware and constrain finger motion, our approach delivers short discrete pulses at key manipulation moments such as object contact and state changes to provide tactile confirmation at the wrist without disrupting hand movement. Two user studies with 26 participants demonstrate that this wrist-based feedback significantly enhances user experience, with 94% finding it helpful, particularly during complex tasks like knob rotation where haptic cues reduced visual attention demands. These findings establish guidelines for integrating accessible referred vibrotactile fee

session name: Session 3: Point clouds, cities, and crowds
paper title: PointShopVR: Immersive Authoring of Large Point Clouds in Virtual Reality
author list: Tianfang Lin, Matthew McGinity, Stefan Gumhold
abstract: This paper presents PointShopVR, an immersive virtual reality system for intuitive authoring and editing of large 3D point clouds. The system supports real-time interaction with dense data by integrating a lightweight acceleration structure and continuous level-of-detail (CLOD) rendering to maintain high frame rates in VR. PointShopVR offers a handful of authoring operations, including point addition, deletion, labeling, copy–paste, translation, and deformation, all with instant visual feedback. A user study demonstrates that users can efficiently complete diverse editing tasks within minutes, highlighting the usability and effectiveness of immersive point cloud authoring compared to traditional desktop-based tools.

session name: Session 3: Point clouds, cities, and crowds
paper title: ScaleFree: Dynamic KDE for Multiscale Point Cloud Exploration in VR
author list: Lixiang Zhao, Fuqi Xie, Tobias Isenberg, Hai-Ning Liang, Lingyun Yu
abstract: We present ScaleFree, a GPU-accelerated adaptive Kernel Density Estimation algorithm for interactive multiscale point cloud exploration. Although immersive environments such as VR support exploration of complex 3D spatial structures, preserving both global context and fine-grained detail across scales remains challenging. We address this challenge with a GPU-parallel adaptive KDE using k-d-tree queries and parallel reduction, enabling ScaleFree to dynamically recompute continuous density fields on the fly and support seamless scale transitions. We demonstrate its effectiveness through adaptive selection and progressive navigation tasks, achieving substantial performance gains and improved accuracy and efficiency in controlled experiments.

session name: Session 3: Point clouds, cities, and crowds
paper title: 360CityGML: Realistic and Interactive Urban Visualization System Integrating CityGML Model and 360° Videos
author list: Tatsuro Banno, Mizuki Takenawa, Leslie Wöhler, Satoshi Ikehata, Kiyoharu Aizawa
abstract: We introduce a novel urban visualization system that integrates 3D urban model (CityGML) and 360∘ walkthrough videos. By aligning the videos with the model and dynamically projecting relevant video frames onto the geometries, our system creates photorealistic urban visualizations, allowing users to intuitively interpret geospatial data from a pedestrian view.

session name: Session 3: Point clouds, cities, and crowds
paper title: Follow the Signs or the Crowd? Effects of Environmental Load and Crowd Dynamics in VR Evacuation
author list: Zheng Wei, Jingchen Gao, Zhe Yan, Yihan Liu, Linjie Qiu, Xian Xu, Yun Huang, Huamin Qu
abstract: We present SAFE-VR, a testbed examining how Environmental Load and Crowd Dynamics influence emergency evacuation. In a preregistered 2X2 experiment (N=80), we found that high sensory load and chaotic crowds independently impaired egress, with the High-Chaotic condition performing worst. High load increased cognitive effort, while chaotic flows drove route deviations and social conformity. These findings support a reliability-weighted cueing model: as guidance clarity degrades, reliance on social heuristics increases. We advocate for "performance-first fidelity,'' prioritizing cue clarity and flow coherence over raw sensory realism.

session name: Session 3: Point clouds, cities, and crowds
paper title: Monkey See, Monkey Break? Study of Rule-Breaking Imitation in Virtual Crowds
author list: Kexiang Huang, Tairan Yin, Jose Luis Ponton, Ruida Tang, Reiya Itatani, Gangyi Ding, Oscar Argudo, Nuria Pelechano
abstract: Rule-breaking behaviors are difficult to study in real-world settings. We investigated whether VR can elicit realistic rule-breaking imitation through a study with 65 participants across four urban scenarios (park walking, unsignalized street crossing, red-light street crossing and queueing). By varying the proportion of rule-breaking characters (0%, 25%, 50%), we found a significant imitation effect: participants' rule-breaking increased with the number of violators. Furthermore, gaze analysis showed that rule-breakers focused on other violators, while followers attended to compliant agents. These findings validate VR for studying social compliance, providing insights for urban design, crowd simulation, and safety policy.

paper title: Predicting Cybersickness Trend and Extent based on FMS labeled Dataset
author list: Jun Ryu, Gerard Jounghyun Kim
abstract: Predicting and mitigating cybersickness in real time is important for VR UX. However, most research has utilized models trained on datasets whose sickness was measured and labeled only after experiencing the content. Moreover, many datasets rely on physiological signals, making them difficult to apply in actual usage. This makes timely predictions unreliable or practically infeasible. We developed a publicly available dataset where the ground-truth sickness levels were densely marked every 0.5 seconds and adjusted/updated on demand using the FMS, while excluding physiological data. We demonstrate that predictive models trained with such a dataset can still produce comparably reliable sickness prediction.

paper title: A Statistical Abstraction Framework for Integrating Heterogeneous VR Datasets in Ordinal Cybersickness Prediction
author list: Jyotirmay Nag Setu, John Quarles
abstract: Despite the rapid growth of Virtual Reality (VR) technology across diverse applications, cybersickness remains a significant barrier. Current cybersickness prediction models face several limitations: most studies are confined to single datasets due to substantial technical challenges in integrating heterogeneous VR sensor data from different hardware platforms, and existing approaches fail to appropriately model the ordinal nature of cybersickness severity scales. We developed a comprehensive statistical feature abstraction framework combined with dual-head ordinal regression, achieving cross-dataset generalization with an RMSE of 0.8341, advancing the development of universal cybersickness monitoring systems.

paper title: Mitigating VR Motion Sickness Through Multi-sensory Simulation of Wind Sensation (MSSWS): A Vestibular-Visual Synchronization Approach
author list: Yuan Yue, Chao Zhou, Tangjun Qu, Yan Hu, Junhao Wang, Juan Liu, Tianren Luo, Xiangxian Li, Yulong Bian
abstract: Users are prone to experience visually induced motion sickness (VIMS) in passive VR scenarios, especially virtual driving. To address this, we propose the Multi-sensory Simulation of Wind Sensation (MSSWS) method, which leverages low-fidelity airflow to align vestibular, visual, auditory, and tactile cues, thereby boosting users’ sense of embodiment (SoE) and mitigating VIMS. We developed a directional wind simulation helmet and built a synchronized passive virtual motorcycle-riding environment. Based on the prototype, we performed a user experiment. Results showed that MSSWS markedly enhances presence and SoE, and reduces VIMS—with more pronounced effects in high-risk conditions involving rapid speed changes and multi-directional movement.

paper title: Kinematic Sickness: Understanding Cybersickness Through Body Kinematics
author list: Carlos Alfredo Tirado Cortes, Yiheng Chi, Juno Kim, Hsiang-Ting Chen
abstract: Postural Instability Theory suggests individuals unstable on their feet are more prone to cybersickness. We tested this in mobile VR by analyzing walking kinematics and cybersickness responses before and during VR use. Vertical Center of Mass movement during pre-VR walking identified cybersickness-susceptible individuals. Spatial stability metrics emerged as stronger predictors than temporal measures, suggesting spatial gait characteristics better indicate susceptibility in mobile VR. These findings highlight the importance of baseline postural stability for designing and personalizing mobile VR experiences.

paper title: Two Phase Multi-Task Learning for Cybersickness Prediction and Adaptive Reduction
author list: A E M Ridwan, Purnata Saha, John Quarles, Rifatul Islam
abstract: Cybersickness is a major barrier to virtual reality usability, yet effective mitigation requires not only detecting sickness but also determining whether and how strongly to apply countermeasures. We propose a two-phase multitask learning framework that jointly models cybersickness severity, blur effectiveness, and blur intensity. Models are pretrained on single-label severity datasets and progressively fine-tuned on a multi-label dataset. Evaluated with block-aware cross-validation, the Time-Series Transformer achieves the best performance. Our data-driven “detect–decide–dose” approach enables adaptive blur mitigation and unifies prediction and reduction in a single model.

paper title: GSReuse: Temporally Adaptive Screen-Space Reuse for Accelerating 3D Gaussian Splatting
author list: Chengzhi Tao, Yiyang Sun, Jie Guo, Tao Zhang, Letian Huang, Junqiu Zhu, daoheng wang, Yanwen Guo
abstract: We propose GSReuse, a lightweight, drop-in accelerator for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) that reuses computations across video frames to reduce redundant rendering. Operating in screen space, GSReuse requires no retraining and introduces minimal changes to existing pipelines. Given the current image, depth, and camera pose, it estimates Gaussian motion vectors and warps reusable content to the next frame. A tile-based filtering scheme then determines which regions can be reused. Experiments on standard datasets show that GSReuse significantly improves rendering speed while preserving high visual quality.

paper title: VRGaussianAvatar: Integrating 3D Gaussian Avatars into VR
author list: Hail Song, Boram Yoon, Seokhwan Yang, Seoyoung Kang, Hyunjeong Kim, Henning Metzmacher, Woontack Woo
abstract: We present VRGaussianAvatar, an integrated system that enables full-body 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) avatars in virtual reality. Our system consists of a VR Frontend that estimates full-body pose and streams pose/camera parameters to a GA Backend, which stereoscopically renders a 3DGS avatar reconstructed from a single image. To improve rendering efficiency, we introduce Binocular Batching, which jointly renders the left and right eye views in a single batched pass. Experiments show that VRGaussianAvatar maintains real-time VR performance, and a within-subject user study against mesh-based image- and video-based avatar baselines reports higher perceived appearance similarity, embodiment, and plausibility.

paper title: Taking Language Embedded 3D Gaussian Splatting into the Wild
author list: Yuze Wang, Junyi Wang, Yue Qi
abstract: Recent advances in leveraging large-scale Internet photo collections for 3D reconstruction have enabled immersive virtual exploration of landmarks. However, existing methods primarily focus on visual appearance reconstruction, often overlooking the interactive semantic understanding of these 3D scenes. Therefore, can we draw inspiration from 3D in-the-wild reconstruction techniques and use unconstrained photo collections to create an immersive approach for understanding the 3D structure of architectural components? To this end, we extend language embedded 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) and propose a novel framework for open-vocabulary scene understanding from unconstrained photo collections.

paper title: AdaEndoGS: an Adaptive Enlightening Model for Endoscopy Based on 3D Gaussian Splatting
author list: Fei Xia, Yiding Wen, Yuanfan Liu, Huanmei Guan, Fei Luo
abstract: To address the impact of dark region on endoscopic diagnosis, we propose AdaEndoGS for endoscopic 3D reconstruction with physically consistent dark region illumination enhancement. The method is based on 3D Gaussian Splatting to construct an illumination-editable 3D scene representation. Ray tracing is employed to adaptively plan virtual light source placement, together with a optimization strategy to ensure harmonious illumination. The VEIDLA dataset is constructed to support quantitative evaluation. Experimental results on the C3VD and VEIDLA datasets demonstrate that AdaEndoGS significantly improves visual quality and detail visibility, providing a solution for endoscopic 3D reconstruction and illumination enhancement.

paper title: GSBrief: A Globally Consistent Descriptor with 3D Gaussian Splatting for Visual Localization
author list: Junyi Wang, Yuze Wang, Wantong Duan, Meng Wang, Yue Qi
abstract: We present GSBrief, a globally consistent descriptor tailored for 3D Gaussian Splatting  visual localization. GSBrief fuses texture and geometry via a structured extraction pipeline and is designed to be scale, translation, and rotation invariant for robust performance. Building on this, we introduce GSBriefNet, a Swin Transformer based regression model with a siamese design to enforce global consistency while regressing point maps to recover ground-truth scale. The predicted descriptors support relative pose estimation, relocalization, and Simultaneous Localization and Mapping. Experiments on ScanNet, 7 Scenes, Cambridge Landmarks, TUM RGB-D, and Bonn show state-of-the-art results across all tasks.

paper title: ForceCtrl: Hand-Raycasting with User-Defined Pinch Force for Control-Display Gain Application
author list: Seo Young Oh, Junghoon Seo, Juyoung Lee, Boram Yoon, Sang Ho Yoon, Woontack Woo
abstract: We present ForceCtrl, a novel 3D hand raycasting technique that enhances pointing precision based on control-display (CD) gain controlled with user-defined pinch force. We introduce a target-agnostic approach for refining raycasting precision, overcoming limitations in human motor accuracy. User-defined pinch force, detected with surface electromyography (sEMG), enables users to easily activate or deactivate CD gain during interaction. We propose three CD gain strategies and compare them through target selection and placement tasks. Our system reduces selection errors, placement jitters, and user workload, especially for distant targets in high-difficulty tasks. These results highlight the effectiveness of applying CD gain to hand raycasting and demonstrate the potential of user-defined pinch force as a robust input modality for precise hand interaction in AR/VR.

paper title: Physics-based Hand-object Interaction via Control Force in Virtual Reality
author list: Yue Ma, Yi Wei, Xiaohui Liang
abstract: Physics-based hand-object interaction in VR/AR is an active area of research. While penetration-based dynamics are effective for grasping, they suffer from significant limitations when handling tiny objects and are applicable only to a narrow set of tasks. To address these issues, we propose a control-based approach that employs PD controllers to estimate force directly from hand motion, eliminating the need for penetration depth and better adhering to real-world physics. User studies across diverse tasks and objects confirm the effectiveness of our method and demonstrate a stronger sense of agency compared to prior work.

paper title: BlanchTouch: Bringing Fingertip Blanch Detection into Mixed Reality for Touch Input on Flat Surfaces
author list: Guanghan Zhao, Yangyang Cai, Kazuyuki Fujita, Robert W. Lindeman, Yoshifumi Kitamura
abstract: We present BlanchTouch, a touch-based input technique for Mixed Reality (MR) that enables interaction on uninstrumented flat surfaces with reduced arm effort. BlanchTouch detects pressure-induced fingertip blanching using a CNN model to confirm touch, while headset-based hand tracking provides targeting information for virtual elements. The system operates solely with built-in headset sensors, requiring no additional hardware or surface calibration, enabling walk-up use in unfamiliar environments. Two user studies demonstrate that BlanchTouch achieves a mean positional error of 5.33 mm and a recall rate of 97.88%, while offering competitive input speed, reduced arm fatigue, and satisfactory tactile feedback.

paper title: SurfaceXR: Fusing Smartwatch IMUs and Egocentric Hand Pose for Seamless Surface Interactions
author list: Vasco Xu, Brian Chen, Eric J Gonzalez, Andrea Colaço, Henry Hoffman, Mar Gonzalez-Franco, Karan Ahuja
abstract: Mid-air gestures in Extended Reality (XR) often cause fatigue and imprecision. Surface-based interactions offer improved accuracy and comfort, but current egocentric vision methods struggle due to hand tracking challenges and unreliable surface plane estimation. We introduce SurfaceXR, a sensor fusion approach combining headset-based hand tracking with smartwatch IMU data to enable robust inputs on everyday surfaces. Our insight is that these modalities are complementary: hand tracking provides 3D positional data while IMUs capture high-frequency motion. A 21-participant study validates SurfaceXR's effectiveness for touch tracking and 8-class gesture recognition, demonstrating significant improvements over single-modality approaches.

paper title: FanType: Intention-Inferring Fan-shaped Thumb Interface for Text Entry on Small XR Keyboards
author list: Guanghan Zhao, Louis Pierre Adolphe Sylvain Teys, GYEONGHWAN YANG, Shengdong Zhao, Yoshifumi Kitamura
abstract: We present FanType, a text entry technique for efficient typing on small virtual keyboards in extended reality (XR). While XR offers abundant virtual space, practical usage often occurs in spatially constrained or mobile contexts where large keyboards are impractical. FanType adopts a fan-shaped layout that groups keys within the thumb’s reach and uses a lift-up gesture for disambiguation, reducing hand movement while preserving natural typing speed. An intention-inferring algorithm further mitigates touch bias to improve accuracy. A user study with 22 participants compares FanType with touch-only and mid-air baselines, revealing insights and challenges in compact virtual typing design.

paper title: FootEyePorting: Design and Evaluation of Foot–Eye Teleportation Techniques in Virtual Reality
author list: Jianwei Yang, Tingjie Wan, BoYu Gao, Huawei Tu, Henry Been-Lirn Duh
abstract: Various VR locomotion techniques have been proposed, including teleportation, walking-in-place, and redirected walking, yet most overlook humans’ natural ability to coordinate multiple modalities. Inspired by eye–foot coordination in real-world walking, this study investigates how such coordination can be leveraged to improve VR teleportation in limited physical spaces. We present four novel teleportation techniques that combine users’ gaze behavior with 3D foot position input. A user study with 20 participants compared our methods against a state-of-the-art foot-based baseline. Results show significantly reduced task completion times, lower NASA-TLX workload, higher SUS scores, and strong user preference for our techniques.

paper title: Evaluation of Visual Feedback Techniques for Mid-air Teleportation in an Immersive Environment
author list: Nanjia Wang, Saadman Rahman, Brody Wells, Daniel Zielasko, Frank Maurer
abstract: Mid-air teleportation extends the Point & Teleport metaphor to support free navigation in three-dimensional space. This paper investigates six different implementations of visual feedback techniques during target specification in mid-air teleportation. We evaluated these techniques through a user study with 56 participants. The results reveal trade-offs between the downward-facing camera, which achieves the highest accuracy, and other visual feedback techniques that rely on occlusion cues, such as the spherical pointer, the spherical boundary, the ground reference cylinder, and the avatar, which result in lower task completion time. We propose initial design guidelines for target selection-based mid-air teleportation based on our findings.

paper title: NinjaPort: A Multi-Hand Approach for VR Teleportation
author list: Bakdauren Narbayev, Patricia Lasserre, Khalad Hasan
abstract: Teleportation in VR allows users to instantly move between locations. Advances in HMD hand-tracking have enabled researchers to explore hand-based teleportation, but it often suffers from reduced pointing accuracy and increased effort. We present NinjaPort, a novel technique enabling users to control four virtual copies of the dominant hand with rays, each positioned at a different distance. Teleportation distance is selected by pinching a specific finger on the non-dominant hand. An initial study explored optimal spatial arrangements of virtual hands. A second comparative study shows NinjaPort achieves faster, more accurate teleportation with reduced hand rotation compared to other hand-based techniques.

paper title: SATOR: Seamless 3D Teleportation to Both Ground and Mid-Air Targets
author list: Daniel Rupp, Matthias Wölwer, Torsten Wolfgang Kuhlen, Daniel Zielasko, Tim Weissker
abstract: Traditional target-selection-based teleportation relies on intersecting a (curved) ray with scene geometry, limiting navigation to ground points and restricting users' freedom. While methods exist for mid-air target selection, they are often inaccurate or cumbersome when switching between air and ground movement. We introduce SATOR, a novel 3D teleportation technique that improves accuracy and efficiency for both ground and mid-air targets by building on previous approaches. In a user study with 30 participants, SATOR proved to be faster, more precise, and easier to use than existing methods, helping users explore environments and navigate obstacles effectively in all three dimensions.

paper title: Teleportation Destination Previews Support Memory Retention During Virtual Navigation
author list: Zubin Datta Choudhary, Ferran Argelaguet Sanz, Gerd Bruder, Greg Welch
abstract: Teleportation is a common VR locomotion technique but introduces abrupt spatial transitions that may disrupt memory. Drawing on the Doorway Effect and Event Segmentation Theory, we investigate how teleportation design influences visual short-term memory. of 3D objects. In a within-subjects 2x2 study (N=27), participants memorized 3D objects and teleported within or across environments, with or without destination previews, before completing recognition tests. Results show that environmental changes impair memory in the absence of previews, while previews mitigate this effect. These findings highlight the cognitive consequences of teleportation design and inform transition design for VR learning and training.

paper title: RSATalker: Realistic Socially-Aware Talking Head Generation for Multi-Turn Conversation
author list: Peng Chen, Xiaobao Wei, Yi Yang, Naiming Yao, Hui Chen, Feng Tian
abstract: Talking head generation is critical for VR social interaction but existing methods trade realism for efficiency or ignore social dynamics. We present RSATalker, the first 3D Gaussian Splatting–based framework for realistic and socially aware multi-turn talking head generation. RSATalker drives mesh-based facial motion from speech and binds 3D Gaussians to mesh facets for high-fidelity rendering. A socially aware module encodes interpersonal relationships (blood vs. non-blood, equal vs. unequal) into learnable embeddings to model partner-dependent behaviors. We further propose a three-stage training scheme and a relationship-annotated dataset. Experiments show state-of-the-art realism and social relationship accuracy.

paper title: What You See Is What You Wear: Crafting Garments for Diverse Avatars with Consistent Wearing Effects
author list: Zan Wang, Anqi Li, yixuan li, Wei Liang, Bing Ning
abstract: In immersive VR applications, avatar personalization requires generating realistic outfits from a single reference image while supporting diverse body shapes, seamless adaptation, and efficient rendering. This remains challenging due to the substantial variation in avatar geometries, which limits the availability of paired training data. We propose Tailor, a two-stage framework that dresses 3D humanoid avatars from reference images while preserving the observed wearing effects. Tailor first predicts garments in a structured, topology-independent sewing-pattern space, and then performs instance-specific optimization to adapt the garment to target avatars. The framework additionally supports intuitive garment editing and real-time rendering.

paper title: Am I Still Me? Visual Congruence Across Reality–Virtuality and Avatar Appearance in Shaping Self-Perception and Behavior
author list: Marie Luisa Fiedler, Christian Merz, Lukas Schach, Jonathan Tschanter, Mario Botsch, Carolin Wienrich, Marc Erich Latoschik
abstract: This paper examines how visual self-representation congruence shapes self-perception and behavior. In a 1×4 within-subjects study, participants performed tasks in physical reality or a digital twin VR while embodying a self-similar avatar, a dissimilar same-gender avatar, or a dissimilar opposite-gender avatar. Subjective measures of presence, sense of embodiment, and self-identification were paired with an objective movement metric. Self-similar avatars strengthened the sense of embodiment and self-identification, while dissimilar avatars induced measurable behavioral change. However, even highly self-similar avatars did not fully match physical reality, highlighting persistent limits of VR embodiment fidelity.

paper title: It's All in the Personality: A Comparative Study of Real, Ideal, and Customized Virtual Instructors for AR Assembly Tasks
author list: Abdul Mannan Mohammed, Martin McCarthy, Carsten Neumann, Gerd Bruder, Dirk Reiners, Carolina Cruz-Neira
abstract: Designing effective personalities for embodied virtual instructors driven by Large Language Models remains challenging. We present findings from a within-subjects experiment that compared assembly performance and user experience guided by a real human and three virtual instructors. Virtual Big 5 personality profiles either replicated a real human instructor, an ideal profile based on pedagogical research, or were customized by the participant. Instruction from real experts provide superior task efficiency and clarity, while instructors with idealized or user-customized personalities fostered higher user engagement and social presence compared to the virtual replica. User customization led to the strongest preference for future interaction.

paper title: Beyond the Self: Avatar Anthropomorphic Fidelity and Calibration Effects on Judging Others' Interpersonal Horizontal Reachability in VR
author list: Vyomakesh Shivakumar, Kristopher C. Kohm, Andrew Robb, Christopher Pagano, Sabarish V. Babu
abstract: Effective VR collaboration requires accurately perceiving others' action capabilities. We investigated how observers judge virtual actors' horizontal reach across three avatar representations: high-fidelity, low-fidelity, and end-effector-only (N=35). Users systematically overestimated others' reachability, but calibration feedback significantly improved accuracy. Surprisingly, visual fidelity showed no effect on initial judgments but significantly affected post-calibration performance; low-fidelity avatars produced better outcomes than high-fidelity representations. These findings advance social perception theory in VR and provide design guidelines for collaborative virtual environments requiring spatial coordination.

paper title: Shape-Shifting Splats: Realtime Context Translation for Gaussian Splatting in VR
author list: Thomas Kernbauer, Simon Fussi, Philipp Fleck, Clemens Arth
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has revolutionized novel-view synthesis by producing photorealistic renderings of a wide range of objects. However, existing 3DGS methods primarily support static scene representations, limiting their flexibility in scenarios requiring visual variation. We introduce Shape-Shifting Splats, a novel approach that enables dynamic runtime transformations of Gaussian splats. Our formulation supports changes in both color and geometry at render time without increasing memory usage or introducing visual artifacts. Once trained, Shape-Shifting Splats generate high-fidelity novel views while allowing flexible modifications of geometry and appearance.

paper title: LIVE-GS: LLM Powers Interactive VR Experience with Physics-Aware Gaussian Splatting
author list: Haotian Mao, Hangyu Zhou, Zhuoxiong Xu, Siyue Wei, Yule Quan, Yan Zhang, Zixuan Guo, Nianchen Deng, Xubo Yang
abstract: We propose LIVE-GS, a highly realistic Virtual Reality (VR) system powered by Large Language Models (LLMs), which enables rapid creation of dynamic Gaussian assets and real-time VR interactions. We conducted interviews to examine challenges faced by current GS-based VR systems and the specific demands of users. Based on these insights, we employed GPT-4o to analyze key physical properties of objects that significantly impact user interactions, ensuring physics-based interactions in VR align with real-world phenomena. A key innovation of LIVE-GS is its ability to predict reasonable parameters in just 10 seconds from static Gaussian assets while maintaining high-quality VR interactions.

paper title: OFERA: Blendshape-driven 3D Gaussian Control for Occluded Facial Expression to Realistic Avatars in VR
author list: Seokhwan Yang, Boram Yoon, Seoyoung Kang, Hail Song, Woontack Woo
abstract: We present OFERA, a real-time framework for controlling photorealistic Gaussian head avatars using only blendshape signals from commercial VR headsets. Unlike sensor- or camera-based approaches, OFERA avoids added hardware, privacy concerns, and limited data access. Our framework comprises three components: Blendshape Distribution Alignment (BDA) to normalize headset signals, an Expression Parameter Mapper (EPM) to drive Gaussian avatars, and a Mapper-integrated Avatar (MiA) that enforces distributional consistency during avatar learning. OFERA forms an end-to-end real-time VR pipeline. Quantitative results and a user study show improved expression fidelity while preserving avatar realism, enhancing VR telepresence.

paper title: Motion Hierarchical Gaussian for Dynamic Control in VR
author list: Runze Fan, Jian Wu, Qixiang Ma, Zhikai Wen, Lili Wang
abstract: Intuitive motion control is essential for virtual reality, allowing users to manipulate objects naturally while receiving realistic and responsive visual feedback. Existing methods falls short in accurate motion control of dynamic objects. We propose a motion hierarchical Gaussian based dynamic control method. First, a motion hierarchical Gaussian representation is introduced and initialized with semantic and deformation information. Then a motion hierarchical decomposition method is proposed to optimize the local motion in the representation. The representation is next optimized by a local motion analysis based refinement method. We also design a set of motion control operations for the motion hierarchical Gaussian.

paper title: Hybrid Foveated Path Tracing with Peripheral Gaussians for Immersive Anatomy
author list: Constantin Kleinbeck, Luisa Theelke, Hannah Schieber, Ulrich Eck, Rüdiger von Eisenhart-Rothe, Daniel Roth
abstract: Volumetric medical imaging offers great potential for understanding complex pathologies. However, existing methods are either low quality, too computationally expensive for VR, or not fully interactive. We propose a hybrid rendering approach for high-quality and interactive immersive anatomical visualization. We combine streamed foveated path tracing with a lightweight Gaussian Splatting approximation of the periphery. The peripheral model generation is optimized in under a second with volume data and continuously refined using foveal renderings. The two modalities are combined on device, resulting in high fidelity renderings with robustness to latency, preserving each approaches strength without extensive preprocessing or approximations.

paper title: Direct vs. Score-based Selection: Understanding the Heisenberg Effect in Target Acquisition Across Input Modalities in Virtual Reality
author list: Linjie Qiu, Duotun Wang, Boyu Li, Jiawei Li, Yulin Shen, Zeyu Wang, Mingming Fan
abstract: Target selection is fundamental in virtual reality (VR), yet confirming a selection can disrupt tracked poses and shift the intended target, known as the Heisenberg Effect. While prior work has focused on controller input, its impact on bare-hand input and score-based selection remains unclear. We present a within-subject study comparing controller and hand input across direct and score-based selection mechanisms. Results show that hand input is more susceptible to the Heisenberg Effect, with direct selection more influenced by target width and score-based selection more sensitive to target density. We introduce weighted VOTE, a history-based voting model that improves selection accuracy by reweighting recent intent to improve performance.

paper title: Exploring Freehand-Based Selection Techniques of Polyhedron Faces in VR Environments
author list: Yifan Qi, Xuning Hu, Xinan Yan, Wenxuan Xu, Hao Zhang, Hai-Ning Liang, Jin Huang
abstract: Virtual reality (VR) enables users to inspect and manipulate 3D geometry from multiple viewpoints, yet most prior selection work targets whole objects. Selecting a single face on a polyhedron is harder because interaction must address a local component while preserving the object’s global structure. We propose a task-specific design space spanning viewing strategy, disambiguation consistency, and interaction metaphor, and instantiate it with eight freehand face-selection techniques. A within-subjects study (N=16) evaluates performance across polyhedral complexity (two radii; 4/6/12 faces), identifying three complementary top techniques and design guidelines. A follow-up study on realistic models confirms robustness and practical usability.

paper title: Slightly Stretching Your Arm’s Reach May Improve VR Interaction: The Optimal Interaction Zone in a Memory Task
author list: Yuke Pi, Marco Fyfe Pietro Gillies, Dorothy Cowie, Xueni Pan
abstract: This study investigates how interaction methods and spatial distance affect user performance and experience in virtual reality (VR) tasks. We compared the Go-Go technique with ray-based Laser selection across eight target distances (0.67x to 4x arm length) in a memory task to identify distance ranges that minimize motor and cognitive demands and support working memory. Results with 41 participants revealed a trade-off: Go-Go enhanced embodiment, while Laser yielded higher efficiency and lower workload. This trade-off was distance-dependent: Go-Go performed best at 1.2x–1.4x, and Laser at 1.4x–2.2x. Our results offer practical design guidelines for adaptive, distance-aware VR systems that align performance and subjective experience.

paper title: Effect of Interpupillary Distance Mismatch on Distance and Orientation Perception in Action Space Across HMDs
author list: Hunter C Finney, Maggie K McCracken, Eric Zhuo, Sarah Creem-Regehr, Jeanine Stefanucci
abstract: Prior work on distance perception under inter-pupillary distance (IPD) mismatch in virtual reality shows mixed results, and interactions with lens type and target location remain unexplored. Using blind walking, participants judged distances to targets positioned in front of and to the side while wearing either a Varjo XR-3 with aspherical lenses or an HTC Vive Pro with Fresnel lenses under matched and mismatched IPD conditions. IPD mismatch affected distance perception only in the HTC Vive Pro and orientation perception only in the Varjo XR-3. A follow-up experiment confirmed these effects were driven by lens type rather than resolution or field of view, with implications for HMD accessibility and design.

paper title: Use Case Matters: Comparing the User Experience and Task Performance Across Tasks for Embodied Interaction in VR
author list: Jonathan Tschanter, Christian Merz, Marie Luisa Fiedler, Carolin Wienrich, Marc Erich Latoschik
abstract: Integrated Virtual Reality (IVR) systems animate avatars by reconstructing user movements. We examined how reduced user-wearable IVR systems affect user experience and task performance and compared three systems: The Captury (markerless outside-in optical tracking), Meta Movement SDK (markerless inside-out tracking), and Vive Trackers (marker-based outside-in tracking with IMUs). In a 3x5 mixed design, participants completed five tasks spanning avatar-mediated use cases to probe the system's strengths. No system consistently outperformed the others. Meta performed best in fast, hand-based interactions, while Captury and Vive excelled in lower-body tasks and full-body pose observation. Results show the need for use-case-specific evaluation.

paper title: Perceptually-Guided Adjusted Teleporting: Perceptual Thresholds for Teleport Displacements in Virtual Environments
author list: Rose Connolly, Victor Zordan, Rachel McDonnell
abstract: Despite its popularity as a locomotion method, the perceptual limits of teleportation in virtual reality remain poorly understood. This study examines when subtle manipulations of teleport destinations become noticeable. In a repeated-measures experiment, participants’ selected destinations were shifted forward or backward across short and long ranges. Detection thresholds were estimated using a psychophysical staircase with a two-alternative forced-choice task. Results indicate that teleport destinations can be altered without detection, with higher tolerance for backward shifts and longer distances. These findings establish baseline perceptual limits for redirected teleportation and inform adaptive VR navigation design.

paper title: How Far is Too Far? The Trade-Off between Selection Distance and Accuracy during Teleportation in Immersive Virtual Reality
author list: Daniel Rupp, Tim Weissker, Matthias Wölwer, Torsten W. Kuhlen, Daniel Zielasko
abstract: Target-selection-based teleportation is one of the most widely used and researched travel techniques in immersive virtual environments, requiring the user to specify a target location with a selection ray before being transported there. This work explores the influence of the maximum reach of the parabolic selection ray, modeled by different emission velocities of the projectile motion equation, and compares the resulting teleportation performance to a straight ray as the baseline. In a user study with 60 participants, we asked participants to teleport as far as possible while still remaining within accuracy constraints to understand how the theoretical implications of the projectile motion equation apply to a realistic VR use case. We found that a projectile emission velocity of 14ms (resulting in a maximal reach of 21.52 m) offered the best trade-off between selection distance and accuracy, with an inferior performance of the straight ray. Our results demonstrate the necessity to carefully set and report the projectile emission velocity in future work, as it was shown to directly influence user-selected distance, selection errors, and controller height during selection.

paper title: Reorienting with the Bare Hand: Gesture-Based Techniques for Orientation-Enabled Teleportation in Virtual Reality
author list: Yushi Wei, Xinru Cheng, Rongkai Shi, Hai-Ning Liang
abstract: Teleportation with reorientation is common in VR, yet design guidance, especially for bare-hand input, remains limited, leading to slower, more complex interactions and higher workload. We propose and evaluate four gesture techniques: Concurrent Wrist Rotation (C-WR), Decoupled Wrist Rotation (D-WR), Joystick Wrist Rotation (J-WR), and Direction Drawing (DD), in a controlled study with 24 participants. C-WR was fastest but error-prone; D-WR improved accuracy but took longer; J-WR enabled precise orientation yet was slow and demanding; DD achieved balanced performance and was most preferred. We derive four design implications and show two application extensions.

paper title: Portals in Impossible VR Spaces: A Taxonomy and Effects on Spatial Memory
author list: Ana Rita Rebelo, Khrystyna Vasylevska, Rui Nóbrega, Hannes Kaufmann
abstract: We present a taxonomy for VR portals, deconstructing them into three main dimensions: World-related, Portal-related, and User- related. Based on the suggested taxonomy, we implemented eight unique portals as a variation of three portal categories: World Integration, Nature Definition, and Destination Visibility. In a user study with 32 participants, we assess the impact of these factors, focusing on spatial memory, presence, and experience. Our results show that World-Integrated portals improve spatial memory and realism, while the portal’s unnaturalness exposure mainly affects perceived realism. The possibility of seeing the destination of the portal had a positive effect on the user comfort, acceptance, and presence.

paper title: Balancing the Virtual Path: Influence of Amplitude, Frequency, and Input Method on VR Locomotion
author list: Michael Nelson, Christos Mousas
abstract: This paper examines how sinusoidal terrain properties and locomotion input modalities affect performance and user experience in virtual reality. Two studies using an elastic balance board (EBB) were conducted. Study 1 tested terrain amplitude and frequency, showing that high-frequency slopes increased completion time, postural sway, motion sickness, fear of movement, and vection, while amplitude alone had a limited impact. Study 2 compared amplitude effects and input methods, revealing that high amplitude increased difficulty and fear, and that EBB use enhanced presence and vection but required a greater workload than a joystick. Overall, terrain frequency was the dominant factor influencing locomotion demands.

paper title: Reallocation of Body Ownership Between Avatar and Omniscient Entity in Third-Person Perspective VR
author list: Jiayi Hu, Kai Guo, Zhongrui Kang, Yuki Ban, Shinichi Warisawa
abstract: Third-person perspective (3PP) VR enables dual embodiment, letting users act through an avatar while operating an omniscient entity (OE) from a detached viewpoint. We investigate how sense of body ownership (SoBO) reallocates between these asymmetric entities during play. A within-subjects study varied avatar and OE involvement across maze and combat scenes, collecting continuous SoBO traces, gaze/controller data, and recall interviews. Results show: (1) SoBO dynamically reallocates between entities across task phases; (2) higher OE involvement strengthens OE ownership, while combat favors the avatar; (3) gaze and control intensity predict ownership allocation. These findings inform adaptive 3PP VR design.

paper title: A Large-Scale Quantitative Analysis of Avatars in VR and AR
author list: Natalie Hube, Alexander Achberger, Michael Sedlmair
abstract: Avatars are central to virtual and augmented reality (VR/AR), yet their representation in research remains understudied. We analyze 14,440 avatar images from 4,658 publications, manually labeled for gender, ethnicity, body representation, and visual style, and linked to publication metadata. Our analysis reveals systematic patterns, including the dominance of realistic white male avatars in VR, more stylized and gender-diverse representations in AR, a post-2021 rise in stylization and diversity labels, and mismatches between gender-related keywords and depicted avatars. Based on these findings, we derive practical implications such as balanced libraries and simple representation checklists to reduce bias in future avatar systems.

paper title: Avatar-Driven Interaction: Constructing Immersive Education in Virtual Classrooms
author list: Yue Wu, Wu He
abstract: Advances in multimedia and computer graphics have enabled 3D virtual avatars, yet systematic investigation of learner interaction remains limited. We introduce the Virtual Classroom Interaction and Modal Assessment (VCIMA) framework to examine the effects of avatar representation, learning perspective, and viewing device on learner presence, engagement, and preference. Five virtual scenarios centered on physical demonstrations are developed to support controlled evaluation of multimodal interaction across instructional conditions. User study results indicate that adaptive multimodal feedback from 3D avatars in virtual reality significantly increases learner participation and sense of presence.

paper title: HybridSphere: Enhancing Hybrid Meetings with Avatar-Based VR Environments
author list: Koji Momota, Shizuka Shirai, Masato Kobayashi, Naoya Chiba, Photchara Ratsamee, Kiyoshi Kiyokawa, Yuki Uranishi
abstract: Hybrid meetings, where local participants are physically present and remote participants join virtually, are increasingly common, but remote participants often experience reduced contextual awareness and isolation. To address this, we propose HybridSphere, a hybrid meeting system that reconstructs a shared virtual reality environment for remote participants. Using a 360-degree camera and pose estimation, the system represents local participants as avatars. A user study comparing HybridSphere with unmodified 360-degree video showed that, despite lower perceived trustworthiness and likability, the seated 6DoF viewpoint was positively received, suggesting that immersive VR can improve remote engagement.

paper title: Streamlined Facial Data Collection based on Utterance and Emotional Data for Human-to-Avatar Reconstruction
author list: Seoyoung Kang, Seokhwan Yang, Hail Song, Boram Yoon, Jinwook Kim, Kangsoo Kim, Woontack Woo
abstract: This study investigates a streamlined facial data collection approach for conversational avatar reconstruction in AR/VR. We examine which facial expressions are essential for achieving photorealistic and perceptually convincing avatars while minimizing data requirements. Using a two-phase methodology, we evaluate utterance-based and emotional facial data and compare three conditions: utterance only, utterance and emotion, and extensive data collection. Results from 24 participants show that targeted utterance and emotional data achieve comparable realism, naturalness, and telepresence to extensive datasets, while reducing training time and data usage. These findings provide practical guidelines for efficient real-time avatar applications.

paper title: Gloss Perception in VR at Long Viewing Distances: Effects of Binocular Highlight Intensity Asymmetry
author list: Kai Guo, Juro Hosoi, Yuki Shimomura, Yuki Ban, Shinichi Warisawa
abstract: A prevailing assumption in VR is that perceived surface gloss attenuates with viewing distance due to HMD resolution limitations. This study investigates this relationship, focusing on binocular luster from specular highlights. Contrary to common assumptions, under physically based rendering, our results show no reliable decrease in perceived gloss with distance. Participants' judgments were more strongly modulated by monocular cues (highlight luminance, contrast, temporal information) than by binocular asymmetry. Moderate departures from physically correct binocular consistency did not undermine perceived realism. These findings suggest computational resources might be better allocated to preserving monocular cues for gloss fidelity in VR.

paper title: Evaluating Cutout Rendering Techniques for Pass-Through Embodiment Using a Real-Mirror Metaphor
author list: Kristoffer Waldow, Arnulph Fuhrmann, Daniel Roth
abstract: Creating a convincing sense of embodiment is vital for Virtual Reality (VR), yet realistic avatar appearance remains challenging. One promising solution is Pass-Through Embodiment (PTE), which visualizes the user’s real body, but depth sensor limitations create visual artifacts at boundaries. Therefore, we evaluated three edge techniques: soft gradient, hard mask, and a novel dithering technique. Combined with a unique real mirror paradigm, we assessed their impact on presence and embodiment. Results show dithered and soft edges significantly outperform hard masks while dithering provides a computationally efficient, perceptually comparable alternative to smooth blending for enhancing PTE in immersive environments.

paper title: AniXDim: Integrating Cross-Dimensional Interaction into Desktop Character Animation Workflows
author list: Haihan Lin, Ye Pan, Nianlong Li, wanjun lv, Teng Han, Feng Tian
abstract: AniXDim is a cross-dimensional interaction system for 3D character animation that unifies precise 2D desktop editing with embodied 3D manipulation in a single workspace. It augments a commodity mouse with 6DoF tracking and adapts a VR controller for stable planar input, enabling near-frictionless “lift-to-switch” transitions without explicit mode toggles. A glasses-free autostereoscopic monitor automatically switches between 2D and stereoscopic 3D visualization based on interaction state. Results show improved accuracy and speed over desktop baselines, VR-level efficiency, and higher usability—suggesting a practical design pattern for future hybrid desktop DCC animation environments that blend 2D UI precision with 3D spatial control.

paper title: Local Surface Approximation Contours for Virtual Reality Stylisation
author list: Amir Zaidi, Ricardo Marroquim, Michael Weinmann, Elmar Eisemann
abstract: Line art is an illustrative technique with a wide use in education and art. While much work has focused on the conversion of 3D models into a line-art representation, there is a lack of solutions for virtual reality. Existing methods fall short due to cost constraints, distracting artifacts due to stereo-inconsistencies, or limitations regarding input geometry. To address these limitations, we present a contour renderer for virtual reality. It operates in screen space, making it flexible, yet it relies on a local surface approximation combined with a registration error metric for robustness. The method is easy to implement and highly efficient even for high-resolution imagery.

paper title: RayFlex: Inducing Weight Perception through Raycast Pseudo-Haptics in Virtual Reality
author list: Yushi Wei, Sen Zhang, Rongkai Shi, Simon Fong, Pan Hui, Hai-Ning Liang
abstract: Weight perception plays an important role in realistic object interaction in virtual reality; however, existing pseudo-haptic techniques have primarily focused on near-field, direct hand interaction. To extend weight perception to remote interaction, we present RayFlex, a pseudo-haptic technique that conveys virtual object weight in raycasting-based interaction via visual displacement and dynamic ray deformation. Two user studies demonstrate that RayFlex enables reliable weight discrimination and enhances perceived realism, presence, and user satisfaction without compromising usability, informing the design of future VR interaction techniques.

paper title: Multi-WIM Paradigm: Comparing Urban Planning Variants with Worlds-in-Miniature
author list: Karoline Brehm, Ephraim Schott, Manuel Hartmann, Bernd Froehlich
abstract: This work proposes the Multi-WIM paradigm for comparing urban planning variants in virtual reality. The approach combines three visualisation modes of multiple worlds-in-miniature - toggle, swipe and juxtaposed - each enabling different comparison strategies. We evaluated these modes in a VR user study (N = 24), examining task performance, spatial knowledge acquisition, user preferences, and collaboration. Our findings reveal that each mode has different qualities: stacked views supported efficient individual comparison, while juxtaposed views were preferred for collaboration and shared sensemaking. Overall, our findings suggest that users would benefit from seamless switching between modes for the given task in urban planning applications.

paper title: Scaled Mouse: An Efficient and Accurate Interaction Technique for 3D Docking in Seated VR
author list: Xinpeng Liu, Di Zhao, Wolfgang Stuerzlinger
abstract: We evaluated mouse-based 3D manipulation as an alternative to VR controllers for seated virtual reality. We introduce novel methods for showing the real mouse position in VR and for mapping standard mouse actions to 3D object manipulation. Results from our first study identified the strengths of the mouse. The second study, with a scaled mapping, shows that the mouse achieves higher accuracy than controllers, with comparable completion times. Also, user workload decreased, and usability increased. These findings establish the mouse as a viable and effective input device for seated VR, offering significant advantages in familiarity and accuracy for professional applications.

paper title: Re-evaluating Virtual Reality Manipulation Techniques for Precise Alignment of Complex 3D Objects
author list: Cherelle Connor, Alexander Giovannelli, Leonardo Pavanatto, Francielly Rodrigues, Haichao Miao, Vuthea Chheang, Brian Giera, Peer-Timo Bremer, Doug Bowman
abstract: Prior manipulation techniques can achieve precise object placement in virtual reality, but studies of these techniques typically use simple objects. We compared AMP-IT and WISDOM techniques, during alignment of an object with complex geometry to evaluate the influence of geometric complexity on the performance, usability, workload and preference. Our findings indicate that participants had faster completion times and higher trial completion rates with AMP-IT on high-precision alignment tasks, contrary to earlier findings with simple objects. WISDOM is still preferred and considered more usable, despite increased workload and poorer performance, exposing participants’ willingness to trade objective performance for comfort during use.

paper title: Interaction Under Whole-Body User Rotations in VR Space
author list: Filip Škola, Fotis Liarokapis
abstract: Many advanced capabilities of VR interaction remain underexplored due to comfort or cybersickness worries. This study examined how altering users' virtual pitch orientation influences interaction performance and subjective ratings. Thirty seated participants experienced 12 virtual tilts from moderate to extreme (±180°). Results revealed no significant increases in nausea or disorientation, with mild tilts performing equivalently to baseline. Even extreme tilts maintained low nausea levels. Performance was better in forward than backward tilts. These findings suggest VR experiences with virtual orientations distinct from physical posture are feasible, enabling novel opportunities in simulation, gaming, and interface design.

paper title: LocoScooter: Designing a Stationary Scooter-Based Locomotion System for Navigation in Virtual Reality
author list: Wei He, Xiang Li, Per Ola Kristensson, Ge Lin KAN
abstract: Virtual locomotion remains a challenge in VR, especially in space-limited environments where room-scale walking is impractical. We present LocoScooter, a low-cost, deployable locomotion interface combining foot-sliding on a compact treadmill with handlebar steering inspired by scooter riding. Built from commodity hardware, it supports embodied navigation through familiar, physically engaging movement. In a within-subject study (N = 14), LocoScooter significantly improved immersion, enjoyment, and bodily involvement over joystick navigation, while maintaining comparable efficiency and usability. Despite higher physical demand, users did not report increased fatigue, suggesting familiar movements can enrich VR navigation.

paper title: Can Treadmill Walking Replicate Natural Walking? Optimizing Speed, Platform Tilt, and Training Duration on a Cyberith Virtualizer Elite 2
author list: Sodabe Bandali, Soumyajit Chakraborty, Andrew McAvan, Timothy P McNamara, Bobby Bodenheimer
abstract: Locomotion is central to spatial learning in immersive virtual environments (IVEs), with natural walking providing rich proprioceptive and vestibular cues. Due to space constraints, omnidirectional treadmills such as the Cyberith Virtualizer Elite 2 are often used, allowing walking via foot sliding on a tiltable platform with configurable speed. We investigated how tilt, speed, and training duration affect distance perception and cybersickness. In Experiment 1, blind-walking results showed that slower speeds (≈0.8×) and moderate tilt (≈8.5°) best matched natural walking and minimized cybersickness, with ≈0.7× emerging as optimal. Experiment 2 showed that ≥6 minutes of training yielded performance comparable to natural walking.

paper title: Influence of Avatar Appearance and Target Distance on Locomotion Method Selection in Virtual Reality
author list: Omar A. Khan, Junyeong Kum, Hyeongil Nam, Myungho Lee, Kangsoo Kim
abstract: Room-scale locomotion in VR can promote physical movement to combat the negative effects of a sedentary lifestyle. Therefore, it is useful to investigate factors which may motivate users to walk instead of teleport. We investigated the effects of avatar appearance and target distance on users’ locomotion behavior, when given the option to either walk or teleport. We manipulated three aspects of avatar appearance—body completeness (full-body vs upper-body only), body weight (normal vs obese), and clothing (casual vs superhero armor), and we provided three target distances (1.5 meters, 3.0 meters, and 4.5 meters). We found a significant main effect, interaction effect and correlation in our results.

paper title: Toward More Intuitive VR Locomotion Techniques: How Locomotion Metaphors Shape Users’ Mental Models
author list: Lisa Marie Prinz, Tintu Mathew, Benjamin Weyers
abstract: Interface metaphors are thought to enable an intuitive and effective interaction with a user interface by allowing users to draw on existing knowledge and reducing the need for instructions. This makes metaphors a promising candidate to drive the development of locomotion interfaces for virtual reality (VR). Since creating metaphoric interfaces can be difficult, it is important to analyze how typical locomotion metaphors can support an intuitive interaction. We performed a qualitative online study to observe the effect of typical metaphors (Walking, Steering, Flying, and Teleportation) and the influence of perceived affordances and user background. Our analysis shows that users adapt the interface expectations induced by metaphors to fit the perceived affordances instead of changing them. Several interests, the age, education, and gender influenced the expectations regarding the VR locomotion interface. Our findings contribute to a better understanding of users’ mental models of VR locomotion metaphors, which seem necessary for designing more intuitive locomotion.

paper title: Collaborative Navigation Improves Spatial Learning Across Symmetric and Asymmetric Locomotion in Virtual Reality
author list: Soumyajit Chakraborty, Holly C Gagnon, Timothy P McNamara, Bobby Bodenheimer
abstract: We investigated individuals and dyads navigating shared virtual mazes using symmetric (same) versus asymmetric (mixed) locomotion methods using steering and teleportation, and assessed their acquired survey knowledge of the mazes. Findings indicate that dyads acquired better survey knowledge than individuals across locomotion conditions. Teleportation showed no significant difference in distance error compared to steering, but yielded significantly lower angular error. Asymmetric locomotion methods did not impair dyadic learning or increase cybersickness significantly compared to symmetric locomotion methods. These results suggest that asymmetric locomotion methods can improve accessibility without compromising spatial awareness or comfort.

paper title: Exploring Mediation by an Embodied Virtual Agent in Immersive Triadic Collaborative Decision-Making
author list: Binyang Han, Ze Dong, Jingjing Zhang, Ruoyu Wen, Tatsunori Hirai, Adrian James Clark, Thammathip Piumsomboon
abstract: This study explored Embodied Virtual Agents as mediators in triadic collaboration, where two users with conflicting goals pursue a shared objective. We tested an XR system with pairs (n=24) in a design task, evaluating agent presence and embodiment. Embodiment boosted co-presence, correlating with interactions that improved perceived collaboration and user preference; frequent interactions with the disembodied agent linked to lower preference. Presence did not enhance efficiency or satisfaction. Qualitatively, the agent used facilitative and evaluative strategies aligning with collaborating and compromising conflict modes, enabling adaptive mediation. Findings highlight EVAs’ value in shaping social dynamics and context-aware mediation.

paper title: What Makes a Virtual Celebrity Agent Trustworthy in VR? Exploring the Role of Stylization and Voice
author list: Yang Gao, Yangbin Dai, Guangtao Zhang, Honglei Guo, Fariba Mostajeran, Frank Steinicke, Lin Li, Tao Yu
abstract: Recent advances in generative AI have accelerated the deployment of virtual celebrity agents for endorsements. However, little is known about how visual style and vocal type affect trust, perceived realism, familiarity, and social presence, especially when generated via AI reconstruction or voice cloning. Using a Sheldon Cooper clip from The Big Bang Theory as a baseline, we generated agents varying style (hand-sculpted, AI-reconstructed, non-stylized) and voice (cloned, synthesized). A 3×2 within-subjects experiment (N=30) showed that style affected all measures, whereas voice affected only realism and familiarity. Video comparisons confirmed 3D gaps and non-linear interpersonal-distance–trust patterns, guiding design and future research.

paper title: Is Seeing Believing and Hearing Trusting? Exploring the Role of Visual and Vocal Cues in Shaping User Trust in VR News Anchors
author list: Yi GAO, Zixuan Guo, Hai-Ning Liang
abstract: This work explores how the visual style and voiceover of VR news avatars influence perceived realism, and how these perceptions subsequently shape trust and news credibility. Guided by the Stimulus-Organism-Response framework, we conducted a 2×2 within-subjects study (N=24) manipulating avatar visual style (Realistic vs. Cartoon-like) and voiceover (Anthropomorphic vs. Neutral). Results showed that visual style strongly impacted perceived realism and trust. While voiceover type did not affect perceived realism, perceived voice realism positively predicted trust. A significant negative interaction indicated diminishing returns when both modalities were pervecied highly realistic. Finally, trust did not significantly predict news credibility.

paper title: Comparing Referential Interactions with an Intelligent Assistant in Virtual Reality
author list: Lina Kaschub, Bado Völckers, Uğur Turhan, Philipp Huesmann, Lucie Kruse, Frank Steinicke
abstract: Intelligent assistants (IAs) in virtual reality (VR) enable novel interaction between humans and AI, where referential interaction is essential for shared understanding of the surrounding space. This paper compares three referential interaction methods in VR (verbal communication, gaze, and pointing) in a user study with 39 participants. Results show gaze performed well in performance (time and number of prompts) and usability (UEQ, NASA-TLX). Verbal interaction was less physically straining than pointing but required greater mental effort. Multimodal interactions and feedback could further improve usability and decrease task load.

paper title: Perceive, Adapt, and Interact: An Intelligent Virtual Agent for Education in Mixed Reality
author list: Naveed Ahmed, Zulaiha Afrah Sadakathullah Shaduly, Mohammed Lataifeh, Imad Afyouni
abstract: We present a novel Intelligent Virtual Agent (IVA) in Mixed Reality for education that perceives the learner’s environment, adapts its guidance to their progress, and interacts with both the learner and the real world. Our multimodal IVA integrates speech capabilities powered by large language models (LLMs) with computer vision for dynamic scene understanding, allowing it to converse naturally with learners and provide adaptive support based on their progress. A Scratch programming scenario is used to conduct comparative user study and evaluate the IVA. Results show the highest System Usability Scale, strongest Technology Acceptance Model correlation, and extremely favorable qualitative feedback.

paper title: Evaluating Replay Techniques for Asynchronous Task Handover in Immersive Analytics
author list: Zhengtai Gou, Junxiao Long, Tao Lu, Jian Zhao, Yalong Yang
abstract: Immersive analytics (IA) facilitates complex sensemaking, yet effective task handover—transferring analytical context between asynchronous users—remains a challenge. We investigate session replay as a mechanism for analysts to re-experience a predecessor's analytical process. Through a two-phase study, we evaluate the impact of viewing platforms (PC, VR), perspectives (1PP, 3PP), and navigation control (active, passive) on effective immersive analytics replay systems. Results show that first-person, active VR replays significantly improve task comprehension and workflow reconstruction compared to traditional desktop viewing. We conclude with design guidelines for leveraging replays to support task handover in future IA systems.

paper title: Situated Brushing and Linking in Virtual and Augmented Reality
author list: Carlos Quijano-Chavez, Benjamin Lee, Nina Doerr, Wolfgang Büschel, Michael Sedlmair, Dieter Schmalstieg
abstract: Brushing and linking is commonly used to visually connect multiple views using highlighting techniques. However, brushing and linking has rarely been used in situated analytics in the context of physical referents. Previous work has assessed situated brushing and linking in a virtual reality simulation of a supermarket scenario. Here, we replicate and extend the previous approach by studying brushing and linking in an actual physical space with augmented reality, while further improving the highlighting techniques. Using a video see-through display, we compare augmented reality with virtual reality. Results suggest that AR performs better in time and accuracy, but the effectiveness of the techniques varies by condition.

paper title: Towards Understanding Time-Varying Spatial 3D Data Analysis with Animation and Small Multiples in Virtual Reality and Desktop
author list: Lin-Ping Yuan, Le LIN, Yuquan LIN, Jun Han, Zikun Deng, Weicong Cheng, Huamin Qu
abstract: The increasing availability of time-varying spatial 3D (S4D) data presents new opportunities to study dynamic phenomena in 3D space over time. While animation and small multiples have been used in VR and desktop environments, a lack of understanding of analysts’ tasks and challenges hinders progress in visualization techniques. To address this, we conducted a study with domain experts, comparing four techniques: VR animation, VR small multiples, desktop animation, and desktop small multiples. We identified the strengths and weaknesses of each technique, as well as key analytical tasks, current practices, and challenges in S4D analysis. Finally, we outlined future research opportunities to advance S4D visualization techniques.

paper title: SPLOCIS – Extending SPLOMs to a Scatterplot Cube with Interactable Shadows for Immersive Analysis in Virtual Reality
author list: Melanie Derksen, Viktoria Diekel, Torsten Wolfgang Kuhlen, Mario Botsch, Tim Weissker
abstract: Scatterplot matrices serve as an initial tool for getting an overview of all 2D attribute combinations of a multivariate dataset through 2D scatterplots. Unfortunately, this approach is not directly transferable to 3D scatterplots due to visual clutter. As immersive VR can enhance data analysis compared to traditional 2D desktop setups, we propose SPLOCIS – a VR system that enables users to interactively filter and select 3D scatterplots from all attribute combinations. Our user study shows that SPLOCIS is a particularly novel and stimulating approach to work with multivariate data in immersive environments, and enables solving classic data exploration tasks in an efficient and accurate way, while not imposing unexpectedly high task loads.

paper title: Occlusion-Free Conformal Lensing for Spatiotemporal Visualization in 3D Urban Analytics
author list: Roberta Mota, Julio Daniel Silva, Fabio Miranda, Ehud Sharlin, Usman Alim, Nivan Ferreira
abstract: In dense and geographically constrained 3D urban environments, visual representations of time-varying building data often suffer from occlusion and visual clutter. To address these challenges, we introduce an immersive lens visualization that integrates i) a view-dependent cutaway de-occlusion technique and ii) a temporal display derived from a conformal mapping algorithm.
