Guided by domain and design goals, we discuss the lens visual and interaction design. A controlled user study compares our approach against juxtaposition and x-ray designs. Results reveal that our lens improves task accuracy and completion time, reduces navigation effort, and increases user confidence, leading to design recommendations and directions for future research.

paper title: XR Module for Enhancing Glove Hygiene Skills in First-Year Chemistry Laboratories
author list: Rezvan Joshaghani, Alessandra Winters, Yanyan Zong, Steven Cutchin
abstract: Extended Reality (XR) offers new opportunities to reduce inequities in laboratory preparation for first-year chemistry students. We present the design and evaluation of an XR glove-hygiene training module and a randomized controlled study with 176 undergraduates comparing VR, web-based simulation, and multimedia instruction. Results show that immersive VR significantly reduced pre-lab anxiety relative to less immersive conditions, with effects persisting after students’ first in-person lab, while self-efficacy remained stable. Findings highlight XR’s potential as scalable affective preparation infrastructure that supports emotional readiness and equitable access to laboratory learning for underprepared and first-generation students.

paper title: EFFICACY OF HIGH-FIDELITY VR THREAT-AND-ERROR SIMULATION FOR COMPETENCY-BASED PILOT TRAINING
author list: Teong Leong Chuah, Ahmad Iqbal Bin Othman, Brian Soon Wei Chiam, Lindy Li Wen Lim, Siew Mun Leslie Chong, Catherine Leo, Vinh-Thuyen Nguyen-Truong, Jia wang Tay, Eng Tat Khoo, Jussi Keppo
abstract: The aviation industry relies heavily on conventional Full Flight Simulators, which limits training capacity. Virtual Reality offers scalable, remote training opportunities, but its role as a complement to FFS requires empirical validation. In collaboration with Singapore Airlines instructor pilots, we developed a VR training prototype for a visual approach into Gimhae International Airport, emphasizing Competency-Based Training and Assessment based Threat and Error Management. An empirical study with 39 SIA Boeing 737-MAX 8 first officers evaluated VR against FFS. Results showed that VR achieved performance outcomes comparable to FFS which suggest VR can meaningfully complement FFS.

paper title: PunctVR: Image-Guided Needle Puncture Training via Scaffolded and Self-Directed VR
author list: Wenqing Liu, Chen Liu, Jiaqi Wang, Yan Zhang, Hangyu Zhou, Zixuan Guo, Aixi Guo, Ziang Qi, Jiannan Ye, Yu Zhang, Qishan Tong, Xubo Yang
abstract: Image-guided needle puncture is challenging due to the high cognitive demands of mental model manipulation. Existing simulators often lack structured learning frameworks. We present PunctVR, a VR system using phased subgoals and 3D visualization in training mode, which are removed in test mode. An evaluation in test mode with 16 physicians comparing standard multiplanar reconstruction (MPR) against MPR + 3D visualization showed that while all physicians improved after training, the MPR + 3D group achieved significantly greater reductions in puncture time. These findings demonstrate that PunctVR enhances procedural efficiency in VR, highlighting how scaffolding accelerates skill acquisition for image-guided interventions.

paper title: U-NEXT: A User–Centered New Prototype for Walking Excavator Simulator
author list: Long Cheng, Gilberto Mazzola, Samuel Knüsel, Michael Schreiner, Andreas Kunz
abstract: Walking excavators are highly complex machines capable of traversing extreme terrain, yet they remain difficult to control. To improve operator instruction, we developed a VR simulator integrating real hardware and physics. Our user studies reveal that perspective is task-dependent: third-person view optimizes complex leg manipulation, while first-person is preferred for general immersion. Additionally, video passthrough significantly improved controller learning compared to virtual animations. These findings provide key design recommendations for future VR heavy machinery training.

paper title: The Effects of Simulated Low Vision on Perceived Action Capabilities in Virtual Reality
author list: Maisha Tahsin Orthy, Jeanine Stefanucci, Bobby Bodenheimer, Sarah Creem-Regehr
abstract: Virtual reality (VR) relies heavily on visual input, which can limit accessibility for individuals with low vision. We examined whether simulated central vision loss (CVL) and peripheral vision loss (PVL) in VR influence gap affordance judgments. Participants judged their ability to step over gaps with simulated CVL or PVL compared to no vision loss. PVL led to an underestimation of stepping ability relative to the no vision loss condition, but CVL did not significantly alter judgments, suggesting peripheral vision may compensate for central deficits. The results suggest distinct roles of central and peripheral vision in affordances and accessible VR design.

paper title: Towards Event-guided Panoramic HDR Video Reconstruction for indoor Immersive VR: A Novel Dataset and Approach
author list: Xucheng Guo, Bing Li, Yuhan Zhou, Han Zhao, Majed Elwardy, Yan Hu, Yuanfeng Zhou, Xiaoming Chen, Lin Wang, Yiran Shen
abstract: High dynamic range (HDR) panoramic video enhances immersive VR experiences, yet panoramic cameras often suffer from limited dynamic range and motion blur. Inspired by event-driven vision, we develop a hybrid imaging platform with preprocessing for event–panorama synchronization, alignment, and HDR ground-truth generation. Based on this platform, we introduce Ev-Pano, the first event–panorama HDR video dataset covering diverse indoor scenes. We further propose an event-guided HDR reconstruction method using weighting-function–based luminance fusion to recover missing textures in LDR panoramas. Experiments show improvements over prior methods, and a user study on Apple Vision Pro confirms perceptual quality closer to HDR ground truth.

paper title: Task Breakpoint Generation using Origin-Centric Graph in Virtual Reality Recordings for Adaptive Playback
author list: Selin Choi, Dooyoung Kim, Taewook Ha, Seonji Kim, Woontack Woo
abstract: We propose a method for automatically generating task breakpoints using an Origin-Centric Graph (OCG) to segment goal-oriented VR activity recordings for adaptive playback. While prior task segmentation approaches rely on manual annotation or are limited to 2D video, our method targets 3D VR contexts. Assembly scenarios are recorded using a structured Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph (STSG), and the OCG captures structural changes around a central object to identify task boundaries. A user study establishes ground truth from user-perceived breakpoints, and comparisons show high agreement with algorithm-detected results. This method enables adaptive VR playback based on user proficiency and task progress.

paper title: Towards Edge Holography via Implicit Neural Representation and Compression
author list: Hyunmin Ban, Wenbin Zhou, Yifan (Evan) Peng
abstract: Holographic displays promise realistic 3D visualization for VR/AR displays, but computer-generated hologram (CGH) remains computationally heavy and difficult to compress. We present INR-CGH, a lightweight framework that uses implicit neural representations (INRs) and camera-calibrated wave propagation to generate and compress high-fidelity phase-only holograms for unfiltered displays. We further compress INR parameters via quantization-aware training and entropy coding. On our prototype, INR-CGH achieves quality comparable to optimization-based baselines while decoding at over 250 fps, advancing practical edge holography.

paper title: E^{2}SL: Efficient Depth Sensing from Event-based Structured Light
author list: Xin Dong, Jiacheng Fu, Yue Li, wenming Weng, Yueyi Zhang, Bingyao Huang, Zhiwei Xiong
abstract: Structured light (SL) is widely used for 3D reconstruction, but frame-based methods struggle in high-speed dynamic scenes. Event cameras provide high temporal resolution, enabling efficient event-based SL. We propose E2SL, an efficient deep network for monocular event-based SL, consisting of a binary embedding lookup table, spatial context enhancement, and geometric-prior regression for fast and robust depth estimation. We also introduce an event-based SL simulator and real-world dataset for training and evaluation. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy with real-time performance at 7.7 ms per frame.

paper title: Record Replay Repeat: Improving Interactivity Between Non-Player Characters with Additive Record and Replay
author list: Klara Brandstätter, Benjamin J. Congdon, Anthony Steed
abstract: Single-user record and replay in VR enables one person to animate multiple characters on their own using affordable, consumer-grade tracking. A limitation of single-user record and replay is that an early recorded character has no information about a later recorded character. We study repeated record and replay, in which one actor builds up a scene with multiple rerecordings. This provides the actor with information about previously recorded characters. In a user study (N=144), participants compared first, second, and third recordings of social scenarios. Results suggest low- and medium-interactivity scenarios only need one recording of all characters, while high-interactivity, close-contact group interactions benefit from two.

paper title: Integrating User Input in Automated Object Placement for Augmented Reality
author list: Jalal Safari Bazargani, Abolghasem Sadeghi‑Niaraki, Soo‑Mi Choi
abstract: Object placement in Augmented Reality (AR) is crucial for creating immersive and functional experiences. However, a critical research gap exists in combining user input with efficient automated placement, particularly in understanding spatial relationships and optimal placement. This study addresses this gap by presenting a novel object placement pipeline for AR applications that balances automation with user-directed placement. The pipeline employs entity recognition, object detection, depth estimation along with spawn area allocation to create a placement system. We compared our proposed method against manual placement in a comprehensive evaluation involving 50 participants. The evaluation included user experience questionnaires, a comparative study of task performance, and post-task interviews. Results indicate that our pipeline significantly reduces task completion time while maintaining comparable accuracy to manual placement. The UEQ-S and TENS scores revealed high user satisfaction. While manual placement offered more direct control, our method provided a more streamlined, efficient experience. This study contributes to the field of object placement in AR by demonstrating the potential of automated systems to enhance user experience and task efficiency.

paper title: Evaluating the Viability of Additive Models to Predict Task Completion Time for 3D Interactions in Augmented Reality
author list: Logan Lane, Ibrahim Tahmid, Feiyu Lu, Doug Bowman
abstract: Additive models of interaction performance, such as the Keystroke-Level Model (KLM), are tools that allow designers to compare and optimize the performance of user interfaces by summing the predicted times for the atomic components of a specific interaction to predict the total time it would take to complete that interaction. This approach has rarely been explored for 3D interfaces. We propose a KLM-style additive model, based on existing atomic task models, to predict task completion time for 3D interaction tasks. We performed two studies to evaluate the feasibility of this approach across multiple input modalities. We found that additive models can predict both absolute and relative performance of input modalities with reasonable accuracy

paper title: AdaptiController: VR-Enhanced Fine Motor Assistance Through Finger Pressure Modulation
author list: Hangyu Zhou, Haotian Mao, Zixuan Guo, Yushi Wei, Yan Zhang, Xubo Yang
abstract: This research investigates finger pressure as a continuous, implicit input modality to enhance precision interaction in virtual reality. Through empirical studies, we reveal a strong relationship between pressure dynamics and precision demands, and propose a lightweight sigmoid-based model to infer desired control granularity. Evaluations in video scrubbing and VR sketching tasks show that our adaptive pressure-based technique outperforms static sensitivity baselines, improving performance and user preference while reducing cognitive load.

paper title: Ubi Grip: Ubiquitous Grip-Based Tangible Object Utilization in Augmented Reality
author list: Xiang Chen, Lei Zhou, Xufeng Jian, Guangtian Liu, Xiayang Zhou, Haifeng Sun, Qi Qi, pengfei ren, Shan Jiang, Jing Wang, Jianxin Liao, Jingyu Wang
abstract: Tangible AR enhances immersion by providing haptic feedback via physical proxies, yet existing methods focus on global object properties and incur switching costs with dedicated proxy mapping. Vision-based hand-object tracking also faces challenges in real-time performance and occlusion. We propose Ubi Grip, a hand-object interaction framework that leverages local graspable features and a comprehensive interaction methodology. It employs lightweight tracking with a hand mask filter and transformation strategy for pose optimization, alongside a user-defined workflow for grip switching and interaction mapping. Evaluations show state-of-the-art pose estimation and generalization in benchmarks, and confirm usability through a user study.

paper title: Aligning Realities: A Registration Pipeline for Arbitrary Objects in Mixed Reality Using Controller-Based Point Selection
author list: David Tim Luca Mertens, Steffen-Sascha Stein, Kristoffer Waldow, Arnulph Fuhrmann
abstract: Mixed Reality (MR) applications often rely on haptic interaction with real-world objects, requiring accurate alignment between physical objects and their virtual counterparts. Existing registration methods frequently depend on external markers, geometric constraints, or complex setups, limiting practical applicability. We present an integrated pipeline for fast and accurate registration of arbitrary physical objects in MR. The approach combines custom 3D-printed add-ons for Meta Quest 3 controllers with a point-to-point matching algorithm based on Kabsch optimization, enabling precise selection, calibration, and registration. The method is evaluated using a modern motion capture system and a complementary user study.

paper title: We Care More About What It Does: Priorities and Responsibilities in VR for Children and Families
author list: Qiao Jin, Xiaoran Yang, Svetlana Yarosh
abstract: Families make key decisions about whether and how Virtual Reality (VR) enters children’s lives, but their voices rarely inform the research agendas that shape its future. In this paper, we aim to center those voices. We first conducted a secondary analysis of prior interviews with children and guardians to distill a structured set of family-perceived alternatives for the knowledge and advances needed for home VR use. We then engaged 46 child-involved groups (84 children, 40 guardians) to rank these alternatives and assign responsibility across stakeholders. We contribute a children- and family-informed VR research agenda and evidence on which actors families view as responsible for addressing these priorities.

paper title: Toward Indigenous Presence: A Radical Relationality Approach for Designing Mixed-Reality Indigenous Data Experiences
author list: Sean J. Dorr, James W. Rock, Vicente M. Diaz, Daniel F. Keefe
abstract: We present Indigenous Presence, a design principle for partnering with Indigenous communities to make computing tools responsive to Indigenous priorities. Indigenous Presence blends participatory design methodologies with radical relationality, a concept from Critical Indigenous Theory, and theories of presence from virtual and mixed-reality (MR) research. Examples come from a six-year partnership with local Micronesian and Dakota communities that aims, in part, to use MR to revitalize and exchange cultural knowledges of canoes, waters, lands, and skies. Five factors for activating Indigenous Presence are identifed: 1) having a community-relevant topic, 2) including Indigenous makers, 3) creating culturally identifiable experiences, 4) centering radical relationality in design, and 5) respecting Indigenous protocols. Potential benefits include higher ethical standards for computing research along with increased trustworthiness and participation in computing.

paper title: Invisible Risks in Virtual Reality: Raising Awareness of Profiling and Overengagement through Lecture and Storyboard Interventions
author list: Suzanne Kieffer, Damien Renard, Romilla Syed, Michel Noutcha, Eloise Yang, Jean Vanderdonckt
abstract: This paper examines how awareness interventions relate to emotional responses to invisible risks in immersive systems, with a focus on virtual reality. Across two complementary studies, we document emotional responses elicited by a traditional lecture and by narrative design-fiction storyboards addressing biometric profiling and overengagement—two risks that undermine user agency and autonomy. The interventions elicit distinct emotional patterns across cognitive, affective, and moral dimensions. Qualitative responses indicate recognition of manipulation, loss of agency, and ethical concern. The results suggest that concise narrative scenarios can make otherwise imperceptible risks more concrete and relatable.

paper title: Consideration of Human Values in Extended Reality: A Systematic Review
author list: Mengxing Li, Yidan Zhang, Tim Dwyer, Joanne Evans, Tag Alshehri, Sarah Goodwin
abstract: With the increasing emphasis on real-world applications of Extended Reality (XR), the field faces growing challenges in creating meaningful, user-centred, and ethical experiences. This study presents a systematic review of human values in the extant XR literature. We report findings on authors’ research motivations, approaches to studying values, application domains, forms of technology, target groups and associated values, and identified directions for future research. Based on these findings, we propose a structured analysis that synthesises key trends and gaps, providing a foundation for future research to advance more inclusive, ethically grounded, and context-aware XR design.

paper title: To be Healed or Hacked? - User‑Centered Ethical Design for Embodied AI in Mental Health Care
author list: David Obremski, Paula Friedrich, Carolin Wienrich
abstract: The global prevalence of mental-health disorders has created a treatment gap. To expand access, AI and VR researchers have developed technology-mediated psychotherapy, but stakeholder concerns and readiness remain under explored. Adopting a user-centered approach, we first ran an online survey with patients (N=152) identifying data autonomy and transparency as primary ethical concerns. For the subsequent in-person VR study (N=90), we manipulated data autonomy and transparency to measure effects on self-disclosure and trust toward an AI therapist. Increased data autonomy raised self-disclosure; transparency had no significant effect. Neither manipulation changed perceived trust, though women reported higher trust than men.

paper title: Extend Your Horizon: A Device-Agnostic Surgical Tool Tracking Framework with Multi-View Optimization for Augmented Reality
author list: Jiaming Zhang, Mingxu Liu, Hongchao Shu, Ruixing Liang, Yihao Liu, Ojas Taskar, Amir Kheradmand, Mehran Armand, Alejandro Martin-Gomez
abstract: Surgical navigation relies on accurate instrument tracking but is vulnerable to occlusion in dynamic operating environments. Augmented Reality head-mounted displays provide intuitive guidance yet face similar line-of-sight limitations. This work presents a device-agnostic tracking framework that fuses heterogeneous sensors within a dynamic scene graph to maintain instrument tracking under occlusion. By integrating measurements with varying accuracy and motion states, the proposed method improves tracking robustness and AR visualization consistency, as validated through experimental results.

paper title: EasyREG: Easy Depth-Based Markerless Registration and Tracking using Augmented Reality Device for Surgical Guidance
author list: Yue Yang, Christoph Leuze, Brian Hargreaves, Bruce Daniel, Fred Baik
abstract: Augmented reality (AR) is increasingly used for surgical guidance, but existing high-accuracy methods often rely on fiducial markers that add calibration burden and limit clinical practicality. Markerless RGB-camera approaches struggle with sensor outliers. This work presents a fully markerless, depth-sensor–only AR framework with two modules: a high-precision, outlier-robust registration module and a real-time tracking module. The system combines depth error correction, human-in-the-loop filtering, robust global alignment, and ICP refinement. Evaluations show superior registration accuracy and tracking performance comparable to industrial systems, supporting both static and moving surgical targets.

paper title: BeamStellar: Beaming Display with Low-Latency, 6-DoF Glasses Tracking via Spatio-Temporal LED Encoding
author list: Jonas M Weigand, Christian Eichhorn, Yuichi Hiroi, Yuta Itoh
abstract: Conventional Augmented Reality (AR) head-mounted displays (HMDs) integrate all essential components within the wearable unit, imposing limitations on weight, heat dissipation, and battery life. Beaming Displays (BDs) address these constraints by externalizing computation and projection; however, achieving reliable low-latency six-degrees-of-freedom (6-DoF) tracking remains a challenge. We present BeamStellar, a BD system that enables low-latency 6-DoF head tracking by combining a position-sensitive detector (PSD) with a spatio-temporally modulated infrared LED array mounted on passive glasses. The system integrates tracking and projection via a co-aligned optical path and performs real-time signal processing on an FPGA.

paper title: Change-Resilient Localization Estimation
author list: Fernando Reyes-Aviles, Philipp Fleck, Dieter Schmalstieg, Clemens Arth
abstract: Indoor localization is essential in applications such as augmented reality or robotics. Existing solutions for localization in static scenes work well even for large environments, but localization in environments with movable objects whose pose in the scene change between sessions remains challenging. In this paper, we propose a change-resilient localization method based on a novel geometric descriptor computed only from geometric primitives. Our method is capable of re-identifying primitives that have moved in the scene. We leverage this feature to update a stored reference model (anchor) of the environment to accommodate the changes, which enables localization that is resilient to changes in the scene.

paper title: A Unified Hand and Gesture Tracking via Offloading Framework for Object-mediated Interaction in Wearable AR
author list: Woojin Cho, Taewook Ha, Taejun Son, Woontack Woo
abstract: We propose a novel object-mediated hand interaction system that enables real-time operation with everyday objects on wearable AR devices, overcoming reliance on bare-hand gestures or external hardware. Our offloading framework integrates transformer-based 3D hand reconstruction with a GRU-based dynamic gesture recognition model to ensure stability during physical interactions. The architecture achieves 92.5% accuracy in 5-fold cross-validation on a custom gesture dataset at real-time framerates. Implemented on Microsoft HoloLens 2, the framework was evaluated within an integrated AR application, offering users an immersive, intuitive AR experience with minimal disruption to natural behavior.

paper title: PRISM-XR: Empowering Privacy-Aware XR Collaboration with Multimodal Large Language Models
author list: Jiangong Chen, Mingyu Zhu, Bin Li
abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) enable flexible content creation in Extended Reality (XR) through natural language and visual inputs, but headset-captured frames often include sensitive or irrelevant real-world information, raising privacy concerns when processed by cloud-based models. We present PRISM-XR, a privacy-aware framework for multi-user XR collaboration that performs intelligent edge-based frame preprocessing to filter sensitive content before MLLM interaction. PRISM-XR also introduces lightweight registration and customizable content sharing for efficient synchronization. Evaluations show high task accuracy, low registration latency, minimal spatial error, and strong usability in a 28-participant user study.

paper title: Toward Multimodal Privacy in XR: Design and Evaluation of Composite Privatization Methods for Gaze and Body Tracking Data
author list: Azim Ibragimov, Ethan Wilson, Kevin Butler, Eakta Jain
abstract: We study real-time privacy mechanisms for eye and body tracking in extended reality and show that naïvely combining unimodal methods is often ineffective. We systematically evaluate paired multimodal mechanisms across casual and competitive XR applications using re-identification rate and utility thresholds from prior work. Across multiple datasets with up to 407 participants, carefully paired mechanisms reduce re-identification from over 80 percent to about 26 percent while preserving real-time performance. We also release an open-source XR Privacy SDK to support practical deployment of these methods in interactive XR systems.

paper title: SemanticAction: A Semantic-Driven and Behavior-Aware Password Framework for Adaptive VR Authentication under Observation Attacks
author list: Tingjie Wan, Yalin Deng, Chunlin Wang, Jian Pan, Zixuan Guo, Xubo Yang, BoYu Gao
abstract: As immersive Virtual Reality applications proliferate, secure and usable authentication remains a critical challenge. Traditional knowledge-based methods suffer from poor memorability and vulnerability to observation attacks, while biometric approaches raise concerns about privacy and practicality. We present SemanticAction, a semantic-driven, behavior-augmented VR authentication framework that integrates scene-anchored directional passwords with gesture-based behavioral biometrics. SemanticAction employs randomized scene prompts and decoy scenes to defend against observation attacks, and a dual-constraint verification mechanism that adapts to cold-start and few-shot conditions. Two user studies provide evidence of its usability.

paper title: Obscuring the `Who,' Preserving the `What': Targeted Eye-tracking Feature Obfuscation in Virtual Reality for Privacy-Utility Balance
author list: Nasim Ahmed, Md Mahedi Hassan, Md Mushfique Hossain, Nazmus Shakib Shadin, Xinyue Zhang, Rifatul Islam
abstract: Eye-tracking data enables the precise modeling of user states, including cognitive load, physical load, and cybersickness in virtual reality, but exposes unique signatures of the user that allow for re-identification and the inference of sensitive demographics via linkage attacks. We propose a novel privacy-preserving model that identifies high-risk features based on predictive contribution and applies selective perturbation. This approach reduces demographic inference to near chance levels, lowering gender detection to 60.0% and race to 49.7% while preserving application utility with only a 9.7% average loss. Outperforming differential privacy baselines, our model offers a scalable solution for secure high-performance VR applications.

paper title: CHOP: Breaking Anonymity in XR through a Novel and Cost-effective Chain of Privacy Attacks and Differential Privacy-Based Defenses
author list: Ripan Kumar Kundu, Brendan David-John, Khaza Anuarul Hoque
abstract: The convergence of artificial intelligence (AI) and extended reality (XR) technologies (AIXR) presents innovative applications but raises significant privacy concerns due to sensitive data usage. This study introduces a novel approach called chain of privacy (CHOP) attacks, which combines membership inference and attribute inference attacks to re-identify XR users without needing access to raw data or model parameters. Evaluating on deep learning-based cybersickness detection, our findings indicate re-identification rates of up to 94% and 97%. We also propose two differential privacy mechanisms that significantly reduce re-identification rates while maintaining high model utility, demonstrating effective privacy preservation.

paper title: Augmented Reality Art Museum Mobile Guide for Enhancing User Experience
author list: Tung‑Ju Hsieh, Yao‑Hua Su, Li‑Sen Lin
abstract: The advancement of augmented reality (AR) technology provides means for next-generation art museum tour guides. In this study, we develop an AR navigation and multimedia guide for mobile devices and head-mounted displays. Visitors follow virtual routes to the exhibits and switch to multimedia commentary mode, pointing to exhibits with their phone camera for commentary information. Two case studies are conducted to evaluate the proposed system. The results show that the proposed system outperforms the guided tour and brochure tour in enhancing the user experience and attracting visitors to explore the exhibitions. This research contributes to the field of AR technology and cultural heritage education by offering a mobile application for indoor navigation and artwork exploring, allowing art museum visitors to actively engage with the exhibits and enhance their understanding while maintaining their interests.

paper title: A trust model for human-machine interaction in virtual reality
author list: Lida Ghaemi Dizaji, Nusrat Z. Zenia, Yaoping Hu
abstract: Human trust in machines is critical for human–machine interaction in virtual reality (VR). Prior work defined a three-layered framework of such trust but indicated two deficiencies. There is an absence of a model spanning all layers to objectively capture fluctuations of the trust; and there is an inadequate consideration of human and machine reliability for the trust. Herein, this study proposed a trust model for all the layers and evaluated this model by considering human and machine reliability. Based on two VR use-cases, the outcomes of the evaluation confirmed the pertinence of the model to capture the trust in the presence of human and machine reliability. The model could enable designing trustworthy and adaptive VR.

paper title: Open-Source Toolkits for Experiments in Virtual Reality: A Scoping Review
author list: Lui Albæk Thomsen, Rico Krueger
abstract: Virtual reality experiments increasingly study human behavior, yet large-scale studies remain hard to build, run, and analyze. We present a scoping review of 67 peer-reviewed, open-source toolkits (2015–2025) that support the VR experiment lifecycle: design and orchestration, multimodal capture and synchronization, replay and analysis, visualization, and remote/cross-reality deployment. We organize tools into five categories and extract ecosystem trends, including Unity’s dominance, the GUI-versus-code trade-off, and growing use of Lab Streaming Layer for time alignment. We provide a maintenance snapshot and actionable recommendations for interoperable schemas, verified timing, durable exports, and reproducible, replayable datasets.

paper title: Which Strategy When? Designing an Adaptive Search System for Virtual Reality
author list: Jalal Safari Bazargani, Fatema Rahimi, Abolghasem Sadeghi-Niaraki, Soo-Mi Choi
abstract: Searching for information or objects in virtual reality (VR) often disrupts user immersion, and existing techniques like pointing or voice commands lack context-aware guidance. This work presents an adaptive VR search framework that recommends optimal search strategies based on situational factors. We introduce a taxonomy of five VR search strategies and a factorial dataset linking task, object, and environmental characteristics to strategy choices. A two-stage study captures natural user behavior and evaluates adaptive systems driven by machine learning and large language models. Results show both adaptive approaches reduce search attempts and workload, improve usability, and enhance overall search efficiency in VR.

paper title: MIFE: A Multimodal VR Immersive Training System for Fire Escape
author list: Weijie Liu, Yalei Liu, Jiaxuan Gao, Zixuan Xie, Qiuyu Fu, Lu Lu, Jian Ma, Kun Li
abstract: We propose the MIFE system, which designs 8 distinct interaction modalities along with a real-time fire spread simulation to further improve immersion through vision, auditory, olfactory, etc. Moreover, we have designed a controller-free and real-time full-body motion capture module, achieving mapping between the trainees’ full-body movements and the virtual avatar. Additionally, MIFE leverages a knowledge graph to provide tailored guidance, adapting to trainees with varying levels of fire escape proficiency. The results of the user study demonstrate that MIFE significantly outperforms self-study and controller-based training systems, particularly, improving by 3.08/10 and 2.38/10 in the performance score of the evaluation stage.

paper title: Latency Effects on Multi-Dimensional QoE in Networked VR Whiteboards
author list: Jiarun Song, Yongkang Hou, Fuzheng Yang
abstract: Networked virtual reality (NVR) whiteboards enable immersive remote collaboration but are highly sensitive to latency, which can degrade user Quality of Experience (QoE). This study investigates how latency affects QoE in NVR whiteboard collaboration by analyzing both pragmatic and hedonic aspects. Through controlled experiments, we identify latency-sensitive QoE sub-dimensions and examine their variation across sequential and free collaboration modes. We further compare VR whiteboards with and without avatars, using a PC-based whiteboard as a baseline. The results reveal how latency impacts collaboration differently across modes and platforms, offering practical guidance for optimizing NVR whiteboard systems under real-world constraints.

paper title: Feeling in Sync: Reducing the Impact of Network Latency on Joint Rowing
author list: Laura Simon, Lina Klass, Anton Benjamin Lammert, Bernd Froehlich, Jan Ehlers, Eva Hornecker
abstract: Rhythmic synchronized activities foster social connection by promoting closeness and cooperation. In Social Virtual Reality, network latency can disrupt this coordination. Our study investigates whether synchrony-reinforcing feedback can mitigate latency effects in a dyadic VR rowing task. Participants received real-time visual, auditory, or audio-visual feedback intensifying under stronger synchronization, while latency was varied. Our results suggest that latency effects can partially be addressed through perceptual design. Providing multimodal feedback is a promising strategy for maintaining synchrony, reducing cognitive effort, and sustaining prosocial effects of movement synchrony, such as social bonding, in latency-prone environments.

paper title: Spatial Affordance-Aware Affine Transformation Between Heterogeneous Spaces for Mixed Reality Remote Collaboration
author list: Seonji Kim, Dooyoung Kim, Selin Choi, Woontack Woo
abstract: We propose a spatial affordance-aware affine transformation method for continuous multi-object matching between heterogeneous spaces in shared Mixed Reality environments. While existing redirection and spatial mapping approaches rely on physical objects and walkable areas, they lack continuous mapping across dissimilar environments that support precise object alignment and seamless locomotion. Our method segments spaces into interaction zones and constructs affine patches based on object adjacency and facing configurations, enabling continuous spatial correspondence. We evaluate paired dissimilar spaces and show that, compared to grid-based methods, our approach achieves broader spatial alignment and richer object matching.

paper title: Supporting Bi-Directional Collaboration with Unmodified Immersive Applications Using AccompliceVR
author list: Ruijun Sun, Anthony Steed
abstract: To address the problem that remote spectating systems in VR lack mechanisms that allow spectators to assist or explore inside VR applications alongside the VR players, we explore AccompliceVR (AVR), a novel streaming and remote collaboration system. AVR introduces avatar representations of spectators overlaid into the normal stereo views of unmodified VR applications, while it also enhances the spectator experience by stitching frames of video from the VR player to reconstruct a pseudo-panoramic view. The system is evaluated with existing VR titles and then validated  through a paired user study with 30 participants. The results shows significant improvement in communication and understanding of instructions between VR users and spectators.

paper title: What Sensors See, What People Feel: An Exploratory Study of Subjective Collaboration Perception in Mixed Reality
author list: Yasra Chandio, Diana Romero, Salma Elmalaki, Fatima Muhammad Anwar
abstract: Mixed Reality (MR) supports embodied collaboration, yet it remains unclear whether sensor-logged behavior reflects perceived collaboration. We propose that sensor signals act as observable manifestations of subjective experience shaped by presence, attention, and social awareness. We introduce the Sensor-to-Subjective (S2S) Mapping Framework and evaluate it through an exploratory study with 48 participants across 12 MR groups performing a collaborative task. Results show correlations between sensed behavior and perceived collaboration, particularly shared attention and proximity, highlighting when system-observed dynamics align with user experience.

paper title: Overdriving Visual Depth Perception via Sound Modulation in VR
author list: Daniel Jimenez Navarro, Colin Groth, Xi Peng, Jorge Pina, Qi Sun, Praneeth Chakravarthula, Karol Myszkowski, Hans-Peter Seidel, Ana Serrano
abstract: Human depth perception relies on integrating visual and auditory cues. In stereoscopic displays, visual depth arises from disparity and vergence movements, while auditory depth cues are conveyed through loudness, interaural differences, and spectral content. We investigate how contradictory auditory cues can overdrive visual depth perception to counteract depth compression in VR. A visual target is shifted closer to a prior fixation, while its sound cue is spatially or spectrally manipulated to bias audiovisual fusion toward the intended depth. This reduces required vergence changes and enables faster gaze retargeting. Psychophysical studies demonstrate effective depth overdriving, and VR/XR application cases further validate our findings.

paper title: Depth Perception Cues in VR Under Sleep Deprivation
author list: Ammaar Zaman, James Baumeister, Ernst Kruijff, Eduardo Veas, Aleksandra Krajnc, Neven ElSayed
abstract: Modern VR is increasingly used in demanding contexts where users remain immersed for long periods, making fatigue difficult to avoid. Because VR controls the visual presentation, depth perception can be supported through augmentation. We evaluated augmented depth cues in a 27-hour sleep-deprivation protocol (23 participants; six sessions, 20:00–07:00) using a dynamic depth-plane interception task with four conditions: no cue, text, coded color, and depth-dependent blur. Text delivered the strongest and most consistent accuracy gains, but at peak fatigue it also increased missed responses relative to blur and color. This accuracy–responsiveness trade-off motivates adaptive cue designs that prioritize reliable responding under low alertness.

paper title: Depth Cue Conflicts Between Real and Virtual Objects Disrupt Visually-Guided Reaching in Augmented Reality
author list: Domenic Au, Robert S. Allison, Laurie M Wilcox
abstract: Depth cues such as binocular disparity support accurate reaching in near space, but augmented reality (AR) can disrupt perceived depth due to focal distance mismatches between real and virtual objects. We examined these effects in a visually guided reaching task in which participants placed a virtual ring onto real or virtual posts at varying distances. Accuracy was reduced for real posts, driven by a systematic distortion of perceived virtual object distance. These distortions persisted during active reaching despite multiple available depth cues, highlighting the need to measure and correct such errors in AR devices.

paper title: Perceived Weight of Mediated Reality Sticks
author list: Satoshi Hashiguchi, Yuta Kataoka, Asako Kimura, Shohei Mori
abstract: Mediated reality, where augmented reality (AR) and diminished reality (DR) meet, enables visual modifications to real-world objects. A physical object with a mediated reality visual change retains its original physical properties. However, it is perceived differently from the original when interacted with. We present such a mediated reality object, a stick with different lengths or a stick with a missing portion in the middle, to investigate how users perceive its weight and center of gravity. We conducted two user studies (N=10), each of which consisted of two substudies. We found that the length of mediated reality sticks influences the perceived weight. A longer stick is perceived as lighter, and vice versa. The stick with a missing portion tends to be recognized as one continuous stick. Thus, its weight and center of gravity (COG) remain the same. We formulated the relationship between inertia based on the reported COG and perceived weight in the context of dynamic touch.

paper title: Occlusion Effect on Action Space Distance Estimations in Optical See-through and Video See-through Augmented Reality
author list: Dakota D Kenoyer-Healy, Matthew Sturgeon, Mohammed Safayet Arefin
abstract: In this paper, we present the first research to examine how different occluding surfaces affect action-space distance estimation using OST and VST AR devices. With 28 participants across two experiments, we examined verbal estimates and blind walking distance estimation methods at five action-space distances. Three occluding surfaces: a checkerboard (high-salient), a black surface (low-salient), and a realistic brick wall texture were considered. We used two commercial AR devices: Microsoft HoloLens 2 and Meta Quest 3. Our findings suggest that occluders do not impact AR object action-space distance estimations. HoloLens 2 achieved higher accuracy than Meta Quest 3.

paper title: Walking in the Wild: Safe and Natural Redirected Walking in Open Physical Spaces
author list: Xinda Liu, Qinyu Zhang, Guoqiang Yang, Yunchen Li, Jian Wu, Guohua Geng, Lili Wang
abstract: Redirected Walking enables continuous VR locomotion in limited spaces, but most methods assume fixed boundaries and struggle in shared areas with moving obstacles. We define Redirected Walking in Open Physical Spaces (OPS-RDW), where navigation must remain safe without a static boundary model. We propose DyCoRe, a learning-free controller that casts OPS-RDW as constrained optimization. Using Dynamic Control Barrier Functions, DyCoRe solves an online quadratic program to choose velocities that minimize path deviation while enforcing collision avoidance, then converts them to redirection gains, with relaxation for infeasible cases. Simulations and studies show improved safety, fewer resets, and continuity.

paper title: Can't Nobody Stop Me! Non-Euclidean Portal Reset for Continuous Walking in Virtual Reality
author list: Ho Jung Lee, Taewoo Jo, Sulim Chun, In-Kwon Lee
abstract: Redirected walking (RDW) lets users explore large virtual worlds while walking in limited physical space, but tight physical spaces still require explicit resets such as turn-in-place, which interrupt locomotion and degrade user experience. We introduce Non-Euclidean Portal Reset (NEPR), which detects collision risk and generates a portal to a short non-Euclidean corridor that users traverse continuously. The corridor smoothly repositions and reorients the user and returns them near the target, preserving flow. In a user study we compared turn-in-place, NEPR, and a hybrid of them. NEPR and the hybrid method significantly improved task performance and user experience, reframing resets as seamless transitions.

paper title: Long-term adaptation in VR: Retention of altered sensorimotor contingencies through redirected walking
author list: Niklas Hypki, Taravat Anvari, Frank Steinicke, Markus Lappe
abstract: Redirected Walking (RDW) alters the relationship between physical locomotion and visual feedback, enabling users to navigate large virtual spaces within limited real environments. While short-term adaptation to RDW is well established, the long-term retention remains unclear. We repeatedly exposed participants to a fixed curvature gain across nine sessions over two weeks and assessed adaptation using blind walking, pointing, and perceptual thresholds. Results show clear retention of adapted locomotor behavior across days, evident immediately at session onset, while pointing accuracy and detection thresholds remained stable. These findings demonstrate that RDW adaptation can persist across multiple sessions and may support long-term VR use.

paper title: Gait-Synced Translation Gain for Naturalistic VR Motion
author list: Fiona Xiao Yu Chen, Sen-Zhe Xu, Kui Huang, Ariel Shamir, Song-Hai Zhang
abstract: Translation gain is a key Redirected Walking technique in Virtual Reality (VR) that scales physical walking to enable navigation in large virtual environments. We introduce Gait-Synced Translation Gain (GSTG), a novel approach that adapts translation gain by synchronizing it with the user's gait cycle rather than applying it uniformly. GSTG leverages the single-limb support phase of walking—when users are less stable and thus less sensitive to external disturbances—to apply higher levels of gain. A user study comparing GSTG with continuous translation gain shows significant improvements in perceived naturalness and comfort, while maintaining low cybersickness, highlighting its potential for more immersive VR locomotion.

paper title: CaliView: Continuous Viewpoint Calibration Using Dynamic Rotation Gain Control
author list: Donghae Lim, Shizuka Shirai, Masato Kobayashi, Yuki Uranishi, Haruo Takemura
abstract: Head tracking allows users of Virtual Reality (VR) to freely rotate their heads 360 degrees while exploring virtual environments. When using VR in a limited space, the ability to physically rotate one’s head is limited to a specific range. To address this issue, previous studies have proposed methods to employ distinct rotation factors for real and imaginary rotations. However, its primary usage lies in redirected walking; thus, it is unsuitable for seated VR. In this article, we propose CaliView, which consistently adjusts the user’s perspective to always face an optimal direction in VR, all while ensuring a comfortable posture. CaliView continuously controls the rotation gain to ensure that the disparity between the present body orientation and the optimal orientation is reduced to zero, encouraging the user to assume a forward-facing position with the target orientation. To assess the suggested approach, CaliView, we experimented to compare three conditions: CaliView, snap turning only (SnapTurn), and hybrid of CaliView and snap turning (Hybrid). The research findings suggest that CaliView functions as a useful reorientation technique, enabling implicit reorientation without sacrificing the user experience. Additionally, this study showcases its compatibility with various other techniques, such as the traditional snap-turn, thus emphasizing its versatility.

paper title: Meta-Objects: Interactive and Multisensory Virtual Objects Learned From the Real World for Use in Augmented Reality
author list: Dooyoung Kim, Taewook Ha, Jinseok Hong, Seonji Kim, Selin Choi, Heejeong Ko, Woontack Woo
abstract: We introduce the concept of a meta-object, a next-generation virtual object that inherits the form, properties, and functions of its real-world counterpart, enabling seamless synchronization, interaction, and sharing between the physical and virtual worlds. While plenty of today’s virtual objects provide some sensory feedback and dynamic behavior, meta-objects fully integrate interactive and multisensory features within a structured data framework to enable real-time immersive experiences in a postmetaverse intelligent simulation platform. Three key components underpin the utilization of meta-objects in the postmetaverse: property-embedded modeling for physical and action realism, adaptive multisensory feedback tailored to user interactions, and a scene graph-based intelligence simulation platform for scalable and efficient ecosystem integration. By leveraging meta-objects through wearable augmented reality/virtual reality devices, the postmetaverse facilitates seamless interactions that transcend spatial and temporal barriers, paving the way for a transformative reality–virtuality convergence.

paper title: This Lecture Makes Me Sick: On Confounding Factors Influencing the Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
author list: Tim Weissker
abstract: The Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ) has become a standard tool for quantifying discomfort symptoms in virtual reality research. Despite its straightforward administration, the use of the SSQ also comes with significant challenges that limit its interpretability. We asked students to fill in a SSQ after having attended a 90-minute lecture of our teaching program. Although students were not exposed to any form of VR experience, the resulting SSQ scores were indistinguishable from VR studies and extended far beyond the originally defined threshold of a "bad simulator". We suggest implications for future experiments involving the quantification of sickness symptoms.

paper title: Mixed Reality Golf Putting: A Comparative Analysis of Golf Putting Performance in Real, Virtual, and Augmented Virtuality Environments
author list: Felix Steiner, Ke Li, Fabian Räthel, Frank Steinicke
abstract: Immersive technologies can enhance sports training, but fully virtual reality (VR) often lacks realistic, multisensory feedback. We developed a mixed reality (MR) golf putting simulator combining real equipment with a virtual environment, testing three conditions: real-world, VR, and augmented virtuality (AV), which displays users’ limbs, putter, and ball via video passthrough. In a pilot study with 36 participants, AV improved immediate performance, reduced task load and motion sickness, and increased user preference compared to VR, while maintaining similar presence. AV represents a practical “sweet spot” on the reality-virtuality continuum, balancing sensory fidelity and training flexibility.

paper title: A Comparative Study of Using Augmented vs Virtual Reality in Collaborative Tasks
author list: Telmo Zarraonandia, Alvaro Montero Montes, Roberto Cuervo Rosillo, Paloma Diaz, Anthony Steed, Nels Numan
abstract: This paper presents a comparative study of collaborative behaviours and outcomes in extended reality (XR). The goal is to provide insights into when to use virtual reality (VR) or augmented reality (AR) in collaborative settings where both approaches may be appropriate, such as scenarios in which some activities can be performed co-located whilst others cannot. Forty participants (20 dyads) completed three collaborative visual analytics tasks under both AR and VR conditions. Interaction techniques and hardware were kept constant, with only the immersive environment varying. The results suggest that VR and AR can be used interchangeably, as collaborative performance does not appear to be substantially affected by the XR modality.

paper title: XR Intergenerational Exergaming: Exploring the Complementary Roles of VR, MR, and Environmental Context
author list: Hongxi Chen, Jia Zhang, Zixuan Guo, Nilufar Baghaei, Hai-Ning Liang
abstract: Intergenerational households struggle to find shared exercise activities due to differences in motor abilities and preferences. Although exergames lower entry barriers, screen-based setups limit outdoor play. XR HMDs can extend exergames into public contexts, yet the impact of VR and MR on intergenerational play is underexplored. Our study developed an asymmetric-role XR exergame, using a 2x2 within-subjects design with 20 intergenerational pairs (N=40), to examine Display Mode and Environmental Context effects on user experience and performance. Results indicate children prefer immersive VR, while older adults favor MR for safety and visibility. We provide design guidelines to enhance intergenerational exergaming experiences.

paper title: Investigating the Effects of Egocentric FPV vs. Exocentric TPV Perspectives and Collaboration Modes on Sense of Agency in VR Teleoperation
author list: Zhonghao Zhu, Kang Yueh, Songyue Yang, Fanlu Zeng, Yue Liu
abstract: This study explores how perspective and automation affect the Sense of Agency (SoA) in VR remote collaboration. We utilized a 2 (Perspective: FPV vs. TPV) × 3 (Mode: Direct, Supervisory, Spectator) design, measuring explicit Judgement of Agency (JoA) and implicit Feeling of Agency (FoA). Results revealed a dissociation: JoA was determined by active modes, whereas FoA showed a significant interaction effect. Specifically, Direct control maximized FoA under FPV, but mode differences disappeared under TPV. Additionally, the link between cybersickness and SoA was examined. These findings suggest SoA is a multifaceted experience shaped by user perspective and roles, offering key insights for VR human-robot system design.

paper title: TerraFirma: A Low-Cost and Effective 360 VR Extension for Viewpoint Translation and Collaboration
author list: Fengze Zhang, Ahaan Maz Agrawal, Voicu Popescu
abstract: This paper presents an approach for extending 360 VR to support viewpoint translation and collaboration, without increasing the acquisition cost. The approach models the ground under the 360-degree image acquisition viewpoint with a planar patch, on which the user stands and walks. The planar patch is connected to the background with visual continuity. A user study (N = 30) shows that the approach has an advantage over conventional 360 VR in terms of anchoring the user to the ground, of user viewpoint translation, of collaboration, and of accurate integration of stationary and dynamic virtual objects into the 3D scene.

paper title: CollabVisAdapt: Spatio-Temporal Context-Aware Adaptation of Shared Object Visualization for MR Telecollaboration
author list: Xuanyu Wang, Ye Wang, Weizhan Zhang, Shuaichen Guo, Caixia Yan, Shuming Yang, Haipeng Du, Wangdu Chen, Qi Wang
abstract: MR telecollaboration enables remote users to collaborate on physical tasks. However, with mobile MR devices, visualizing shared objects struggles to simultaneously optimize spatiality, fidelity, and real-time performance. Existing methods explore integrating multiple representations but focus on fixed combinations without considering contexts and automatic switching. We propose adapting object visualization based on spatio-temporal contexts. We examine the impact of spatial contexts on users’ prioritized aspects of visualization and explore temporal switching when the preferred representation is not accessible. We implement the CollabVisAdapt MR telecollaboration prototype and evaluate the proposed adaptive visualization and the system.

paper title: Scalable Collocated Multi-User VR Through Virtual Environment User Spatial Coherence
author list: Yuqi Zhou, Shuqi Liao, Yufeng Chen, Sonia Fahmy, Voicu Popescu
abstract: This paper presents an approach to alleviating the server–client communication bottleneck in collocated multi-user virtual reality (VR). Instead of transmitting unique packets to each client, the system multicasts the same packets to all users, combining packetization, visibility-aware prioritization, and lightweight acknowledgments to ensure efficiency and fairness. To enable rapid scene completion, the environment is partitioned into independent fixed-size packets that can be decoded immediately upon arrival, and transmission order is guided by precomputed visibility footprints. Scalability is achieved through repeated multicast until compact bitmap acknowledgments confirm reception, keeping communication cost mostly independent of the nu

paper title: When Effort Becomes Visible: Facet-Level Shifts in Evaluation and Workload during VR Teamwork
author list: Zheng Wei, Lee Hyeonmin, Yawei Zhao, Junxiang LIAO, Hao Li, Xian Xu, Hayoung Oh, Lik-Hang Lee, Wai Tong, Pan Hui
abstract: Surfacing peer workload reshapes VR collaboration, yet prior work often conflates social and environmental cues. Using TRACE-VR, we isolated these channels in a 2X2 study (n=32) manipulating effort identifiability and peer pace. We found these factors act as partial substitutes: accountability compensates for low pace, while high pace offsets anonymity. Crucially, these cues functioned as "threshold movers," determining who finishes rather than how fast finishers move. While global workload remained stable, specific combinations shifted perceived monitoring and temporal demand. We frame identifiability and pace as substitutable design levers for tempo-aware collaboration.

paper title: Evaluating Preattentive Features for Detecting Changes in Virtual Environments
author list: DongHoon Kim, Isaac Cho
abstract: This study investigates the impact of visual features and spatial proximity on change detection in immersive VR. Results demonstrate that preattentive processing significantly enhances performance when objects are spatially isolated, thereby reducing cognitive load. Conversely, perceptual grouping with visually similar elements hinders detection. These findings suggest that VR designers should strategically utilize spatial isolation to improve user experience and facilitate accurate change recognition in complex 3D environments.

paper title: Semantic Anchors: How Object Familiarity and Rotational Context Shape Rotation Perception in VR
author list: Alston Lantian Xu, Pingchuan Ke, Alvaro Cassinelli
abstract: We propose "Semantic Anchors" to demonstrate that Redirected Walking (RDW) thresholds are not fixed sensory limits but are modulated by scene plausibility. Our psychophysical experiment reveals that object familiarity fundamentally alters spatial cue integration, overriding geometric cues. Specifically, violating canonical size expectations for familiar objects expanded the window for imperceptible redirection, allowing rotation gains up to 30% to pass unnoticed. These findings indicate that RDW thresholds are co-constructed by sensory input and cognitive expectations, offering a principled basis for designing semantically aware locomotion systems.

paper title: Stiffness Discrimination Thresholds of Force-Feedback Gloves for Volumetric Data Exploration in Virtual Reality
author list: Qisong Wang, Per Ola Kristensson
abstract: We measured stiffness discrimination thresholds using Dexmo force-feedback gloves through psychophysical experiments with 23 participants exploring virtual volumetric data. Our dual-method approach revealed Just Noticeable Difference thresholds of 48.1% (Low Reference) and 26.3% (High Reference)—far exceeding the 10–20% achievable with natural touch. A 1.8:1 Weber fraction ratio indicates perceptual scaling violations at low forces. Spatial analysis showed that mid-reach positioning improves accuracy by 7% compared to workspace extremes. These findings establish critical benchmarks for haptic VR and provide evidence-based guidelines for designing applications within current technological constraints.

paper title: Empirically Evaluating the Effects of Calibration Perturbations on the Near-field Size Perception of Graspable Tangible Objects in VR
author list: Alexandre Gomes de Siqueira, Roshan Venkatakrishnan, Rohith Venkatakrishnan, Ayush Bhargava, Kathryn Lucaites, Hannah Solini, Moloud Nasiri, Andrew Robb, Christopher Pagano, Brygg Ullmer, Sabarish V. Babu
abstract: Virtual Reality (VR) increasingly incorporates tangible objects for training and interaction, yet users often overestimate object size. Prior work suggests calibration can alter size perception. This study examined carryover calibration effects on perceived dial sizes in VR. Participants wearing head-mounted displays experienced divergent calibration conditions, compared to a convergent baseline. Results show significant calibration effects on size estimation accuracy. Greater overestimation occurred in haptics-only than in vision-only conditions. Estimations worsened when haptics were added during calibration but improved when vision was added to haptics-only calibration, underscoring the role of calibration in size perception accuracy.

paper title: PerSiVal: On-Body AR Visualization of Biomechanical Arm Simulations
author list: Xingyao Yu, David Rosin, Johannes Kässinger, Benjamin Lee, Frank Dürr, Christian Becker, Oliver Röhrle, Michael Sedlmair
abstract: In this work, we explore different combinations of techniques for an interactive, on-body visualization in augmented reality (AR) of an upper arm muscle simulation model. In terms of data, we focus on a continuum-mechanical simulation model involving five different muscles of the human upper arm, with physiologically realistic geometry. In terms of use cases, we focus on the immersive illustration, education, and dissemination of such simulation models. We describe the process of developing six on-body visualization prototypes over a period of five years. For each prototype, we employed different types of motion capture, AR display technologies, and visual encoding approaches, and gathered feedback throughout outreach activities. We reflect on the development of the individual prototypes and summarize lessons learned of our exploration process into the design space of situated on-body visualization.

paper title: Visual and Somatosensory Integration with Higher Sitting Posture Enhances the Sense of Standing and Self-motion in Seated VR
author list: Daiki Hagimori, Naoya Isoyama, Monica Perusquía‑Hernández, Shunsuke Yoshimoto, Hideaki Uchiyama, Nobuchika Sakata, Kiyoshi Kiyokawa
abstract: Users are often seated in the real environment, while their virtual avatars either remain standing stationary or move in virtual reality (VR). This creates posture inconsistencies between the real and virtual embodiment representations. The relationship between posture consistency in locomotion techniques and sense of presence in VR is still unclear. This study investigates how visual and somatosensory integration affects the sense of standing (SoSt) and the sense of self-motion (SoSm) when the sitting posture is varied slightly, including highlighting the importance of sitting posture for locomotion design in VR. The degree and occurrence of SoSt and SoSm were assessed by subjective experiments, and it was found that higher sitting and lower sitting postures present higher SoSt and lower SoSm, respectively. Invocation of SoSt also influences postural perception. Perception of travel distance varied according to the posture condition when identical visual flow was presented. The findings suggest that visual and somatosensory integration related to posture enhances SoSt and SoSm, and a sitting posture with a higher seating position is recommended in seated VR locomotion design.

paper title: Resisting the Avatar: Motor Responses to Spatial Distortions in Upper- and Lower-Limb Reaching Without Visual Targets
author list: Iris Willaert, Rachid Aissaoui, Valentin Vallageas, David R. Labbe
abstract: Avatars can serve as virtual representations of a user’s body, replicating their movements in real time within an immersive environment. When these movements are closely synchronized with those of the user, a sense of embodiment over the avatar is often experienced. Recent research shows that introducing spatial offsets between a user’s real movements and those of their avatar can alter motor behavior, with users aligning with the distorted avatar. While previous studies have typically focused on upper-limb movements toward visual targets, it remains unclear how users respond to distortions without such targets, particularly during lower-limb tasks where postural demands may impose constraints. This study examined whether users adjust their movements to follow their distorted avatar during upper- and lower-limb reaching without visual targets. Twenty-four participants performed reaching tasks while their avatar’s movements were spatially offset. Results revealed that participants counteracted the avatar’s distortion by moving in the opposite direction in both limb tasks. These findings suggest that, in the absence of explicit visual targets, participants treat spatial distortions as execution errors and prioritize internal movement goals over minimizing sensory conflict. This suggests that adaptation depends not only on a strong embodiment but also on whether the task performance permits reducing the mismatch between the user’s and the avatar’s movements.

paper title: Together in Sync: Assessing Prosocial Behavior with a Full-Body Interactive Mixed Reality Experience
author list: Sena Beste Ercan, Belén Gonzalez, Olga Gali, Gray Atherton, Liam Cross, Narcis Pares
abstract: This study examines how interpersonal synchrony (IPS) in full-body interactive Mixed Reality (MR) environments influences children’s prosocial behavior. We present DragonIce 2.0, a two-part MR game designed to elicit synchronous or asynchronous group activity. A comparative study with children aged 9-10 assessed changes in affiliation and collaboration using system logs and self-report measures. Results emphasize the role of task structure, perceived coordination, and interaction context in shaping social outcomes. The findings inform the design of immersive MR experiences that foster social connection, collaboration, and group engagement among children.

paper title: Effects of Postures on Identifying Users for Selection-based Behavioral Authentication in Virtual Reality
author list: Guanyu Ye, Tingjie Wan, Huawei Tu, Jian Weng, BoYu Gao
abstract: Behavioral authentication is a promising approach for Virtual Reality, yet prior work rarely considers posture variability during use. This study investigates how posture affects authentication performance with a target-selection task. Behavioral trajectory data were analyzed with multivariate time-series classification across three postures. Results show reclining yielded the highest within-posture accuracy despite longer task times, with significant interactions between posture and target vertical layout. Cross-posture authentication performed best when transferring from sitting to standing or reclining. Mixed-posture training further improved robustness, offering practical guidance for posture-resilient VR authentication design.

paper title: Evaluating the Usability of Microgestures for Text Editing Tasks in Virtual Reality
author list: Xiang Li, Wei He, Per Ola Kristensson
abstract: As virtual reality (VR) continues to evolve, traditional input methods such as handheld controllers and gesture systems often face challenges with precision, social accessibility, and user fatigue. These limitations motivate the exploration of microgestures, which promise more subtle, ergonomic, and device-free interactions. We introduce microGEXT, a lightweight microgesture-based system designed for text editing in VR without external sensors, which utilizes small, subtle hand movements to reduce physical strain compared to standard gestures. We evaluated microGEXT in three user studies. In Study 1 (N=20), microGEXT reduced overall edit time and fatigue compared to a ray-casting + pinch menu baseline, the default text editing approach in commercial VR systems. Study 2 (N=20) found that microGEXT performed well in short text selection tasks but was slower for longer text ranges. In Study 3 (N=10), participants found microGEXT intuitive for open-ended information-gathering tasks. Across all studies, microGEXT demonstrated enhanced user experience and reduced physical effort, offering a promising alternative to traditional VR text editing techniques.

session name: Session 32: Impacts of xR design decisions
paper title: Aligning Gamification with Learner Motivation: Insights from VR-Based Learning Tasks
author list: yuxuan bai, yuzhu wang, Pan Hui, Yuyang Wang
abstract: Virtual reality is widely used in education, yet motivational mechanisms in VR learning remain underexplored. Grounded in Self-Determination Theory, this study examines how gamification and motivational framing shape VR learning experience. Gamified and non-gamified versions of a batik-based and a code-based VR task were designed. Results indicate that gamification primarily enhances learners’ immediate experiential outcomes in VR, including curiosity and satisfaction, while increasing perceived workload. In contrast, motivational orientation mainly shapes self-efficacy and perceived cognitive demand. These findings suggest that effective VR learning design should balance experiential engagement and motivational support.

session name: Session 32: Impacts of xR design decisions
paper title: Telling Us What You Experience: Effects of Questionnaire Interface Design on Subjective Measurements in Virtual Reality
author list: Lucas Küntzer, Martin Feick, Max Benzschawel, Naz Mokhamed Al Kassm, Tilo Mentler, Heike Spaderna, Robert J Teather, Georg Rock
abstract: Virtual reality (VR) enables immersive, tightly controlled experiments but complicates subjective measurement: moving participants out of the virtual environment (VE) changes context and can increase recall bias. In-VR questionnaires (inVRQs) support immediate, efficient self-reports and repeated sampling, yet adoption is limited by concerns about biasing experiential measures. In a within-subject study (n=43), participants completed post-task questionnaires using three 2D inVRQ interfaces: a body-anchored watch (direct touch), a world-anchored station (handheld pointer), and a world-anchored board at 5 m (laser pointer). Usability and task load differed, but presence and flow were practically equivalent across interface conditions.

session name: Session 32: Impacts of xR design decisions
paper title: Understanding the Interplay of Cue Design, User Characteristics, and Environment on Gaze and Navigation in Mixed Reality
author list: Yu Zhao, Jeanine Stefanucci, Sarah Creem-Regehr, Bobby Bodenheimer
abstract: Designing effective navigation systems in mixed reality (MR) requires understanding how virtual cues interact with user characteristics and the environment. This study examined two design factors: cue frame of reference (world-fixed vs. screen-fixed) and landmark annotations. We studied navigation behavior, gaze patterns, and spatial learning in 81 participants in a virtual urban environment. Results show that world-fixed cues aligned better with natural gaze behavior, landmark cues enhanced learning, particularly for users with lower spatial ability, and the frequency of utilizing global landmarks enhance spatial memory. Gender differences in gaze–head coordination were also observed. These findings inform adaptive MR navigation design.

session name: Session 32: Impacts of xR design decisions
paper title: Towards VR Training Adapted to Affective and Cognitive States: General Method and Evaluation with Mental Workload
author list: Emilie Hummel, Mélanie Cogne, Anatole Lécuyer, Marie Lange, Valérie Gouranton
abstract: This paper introduces an adaptive virtual reality (VR) method integrating affective and cognitive states (ACS) for personalized training. Unlike performance-only approaches, our method adjusts task difficulty based on both performance and ACS. A user study (N=30) compared our adaptation to a performance-only baseline, with an implementation of our method focusing on the adaptation of the difficulty of a cognitive task based on performance and mental workload. This study reveals a 10.7% reduction in mental workload, a 22.8% performance increase, and improved user experience. These results demonstrate the benefits of ACS integration in adaptive VR, enhancing user engagement and efficiency while aligning with VR training objectives.

session name: Session 32: Impacts of xR design decisions
paper title: How Much Is Too Much? Comfort Envelopes for Distortions in Virtual Reality Interaction
author list: Hayeon Kim, In-Kwon Lee
abstract: VR compels by bending reality, yet principled limits remain undefined. We propose a dual-axis framework distinguishing continuous metric deviations (stretching) from discrete topological breaks (cutting). Through a psychophysical study (N=52), we map the Comfort Envelope, a multi-dimensional budget of acceptable distortion. Results show users tolerate stretching far better than cuts, while predictability expands the usable budget by about 35%. Crucially, these distortions interact contractively, competing for user tolerance. We provide actionable thresholds to help designers balance agency and presence without breaking the experience.

paper title: Assessing the Readiness of Augmented Reality for Industrial Assembly: A Deployment Study Comparing Immersive and Non-Immersive Solutions
author list: Xinyi Tu, Benedikt Hartmann, Per Ola Kristensson, John Liu, Thomas Bohné, Slawomir Konrad Tadeja
abstract: Integrating augmented reality (AR) into industrial assembly processes has great potential, yet most studies use simplified lab tasks and rarely benchmark AR against commercial systems, limiting their industrial relevance. To address this gap, we present a comparative study of immersive AR and non-immersive touchscreen-based assembly support systems on a manufacturing shop floor, with 16 participants assembling two industry-grade assets of different complexity. The results reveal that while AR's immersive capabilities excelled in spatial guidance for complex tasks, the simplicity and reliability of the touchscreen interface proved more effective for simple assemblies. We derive four design implications for deployment practice.

paper title: From Prototype to Production: Case Studies Informing Design Guidelines for Industrial AR
author list: Nicole Tobisková, Sambit Pradhan, Lennart Malmsköld, Fredrik Danielsson
abstract: Over the past three years, we collaborated with Swedish industries to explore how Augmented Reality (AR) can support production workflows in industrial manufacturing environments. This paper reflects on the design, development, and testing of AR prototypes for head-mounted optical see-through displays in real-world industrial settings. We investigate AR deployment across four manufacturing contexts: house production, inspection, tool change, and changeover operations. Through iterative prototyping and cross-case analysis, we identify recurring usability and integration challenges and extract corresponding user needs. These insights inform a set of design guidelines for AR in industrial production.

paper title: AI-Powered Conversational Assistance in Augmented Reality for Multi-Step Tasks
author list: Juliana Hildegard Madritsch, Tomislav Duricic, Neven ElSayed, Simone Kopeinik, Eduardo Veas
abstract: Augmented Reality (AR) systems can enhance performance in complex industrial tasks by overlaying digital instructions onto the physical world. Reusing approved technical documentation reduces authoring effort and ensures compliance. This paper investigates integrating a Large Language Model (LLM) as a conversational assistant within a static AR manual. An A/B study (N=36) using HoloLens 2 compared a PDF-based AR manual with a version augmented by conversational assistance. Tasks included linear, non-linear troubleshooting, and task-handoff scenarios. While total completion times increased, AI-assisted users spent less active working time. Eye-tracking results indicate improved attentional focus. System latency remains the primary barrier.

paper title: IntentionAR: An Intention-Driven Camera–Projector System for AR Assembly Guidance
author list: Xin Cao, Wenbo Shi, Mingyu Ma, Shanhao Yang, Kang Xie, xibin song, Fan Zhong, Hai-Ning Liang, Xueying Qin
abstract: We present IntentionAR, an intention-driven camera–projector AR assembly guidance system to address limited understanding of user intention and complex hand–part interactions in nonlinear assembly workflows. The system integrates online action-based intention inference with a finite-state assembly machine to identify interaction actions and target objects ahead of each assembly step. It highlights candidate parts and flags correct or incorrect selections in real time, while dynamically switching its registration and visualization models to support nonlinear progress and improve robustness and assembly efficiency.

paper title: From Structure to Semantics: Hypergraph‑Based AR Assembly Guidance with LLM‑Mediated Narration
author list: Xinda Liu, Bowei Zhang, Jiaju Xu, Jian Wu, Guohua Geng, Lili Wang
abstract: Effective AR assembly guidance faces challenges in representing procedural logic and managing cognitive load. We propose a framework combining an Assembly Hypergraph to capture hierarchical constraints and an LLM-Mediated Narration workflow to translate machine-optimal paths into ergonomic instructions. A user study reveals a progressive enhancement: the hypergraph representation alone improves objective metrics (task time, errors), while the subsequent LLM narration maintains these gains while significantly lowering cognitive load and elevating usability. This demonstrates the value of using LLMs as a mediation layer for superior AR user experiences.

paper title: Clean Up Your Lab: The Impact of Technology Clutter and Replica Environments on Cognitive Performance
author list: Jack Schwanewede, Alesha Bond, Tabitha C. Peck
abstract: This work investigates the impact of orderliness within physical and virtual environments on cognitive performance. We conducted two experiments where participants entered one of two physical laboratory spaces (clean or technology-filled) and completed a cognitive task in three virtual environments: a MR environment, a virtual clean environment, and a virtual technology-filled environment. Participants perform better in clean environments compared to cluttered. Additionally, overall participants are more accurate when physical and virtual spaces were the same compared to different. Additional results suggest that gender differences may exist with implications for transitional virtual environments.

paper title: Tag-Along Virtual Windows Increase Perceived Resistance and Task Load in Augmented Reality
author list: Motoki Kagami, Yuta Kataoka, Yutaro Hirao, Monica Perusquia-Hernandez, Satoshi Hashiguchi, Hideaki Uchiyama, Kiyoshi Kiyokawa, Shohei Mori
abstract: Head-following AR windows may evoke perceived resistance despite having no physical mass. We investigated perceived resistance in head-based interaction using a within-subject study with 24 participants, manipulating follow-up delay, window size, and UI type (Head-Locked vs. Tag-Along). We measured resistance ratings, NASA-TLX (Raw Scores), and gaze–head angular offset. Tag-Along and moderate-to-long delays increased perceived resistance and workload. Window size showed no main effect on resistance but interacted with UI type. We discuss design implications for responsive and comfortable head-following windows in AR.

paper title: The Influence of Environmental Fidelity on Virtual Presence, Intrinsic Motivation, Cognitive Load and Learning Outcomes in Medical VR
author list: Danny Schott, Matthias Kunz, Claudia Schrader, Elias Ringler, Alexander Schwadtke, Jonas Mandel, Patrick Müller, Constantin Kleinbeck, Daniel Roth, Anne Albrecht, Rüdiger Braun-Dullaeus, Christian Hansen
abstract: This study investigates how scene design in immersive VR learning environments influences presence, motivation, cognitive load, and learning. Eighty-seven medical students learned embryonic heart development in one of three VR scenes: a Blank Scene, a Reconstructed Classroom, or an Inside-Human Scene. Results show that scene design significantly affected physical and self-presence, cognitive load, and comprehension. The Inside-Human Scene supported higher presence and comprehension, while the Reconstructed Classroom was associated with higher extraneous cognitive load. No differences were found for task performance or factual recall. The findings highlight the importance of perceptual coherence over visual realism in educational VR.

paper title: The Role of Cognitive Load and Engagement in Students’ Adoption of an AI-supported VR Training System
author list: Jhon A Bueno-Vesga, Xinhao Xu, Yuanyuan Gu, Hao He, Shangman Li, Yupei Duan
abstract: This mixed-methods study applied the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) to examine the adoption of an AI-supported VR system for nursing patient management training. It investigated how cognitive engagement and dimensions of cognitive load influence perceived usefulness (PU) and perceived ease of use (PEOU). Quantitative results showed that engagement and PEOU significantly predicted PU, while engagement and frustration predicted PEOU. Interviews revealed that frustration was not always detrimental; instead, it often increased engagement by enhancing realism and perceived value for skill development.
